name: Braemar Castle
description: A turreted fairy-tale fortress in the heart of Royal Deeside, wrapped in a star-shaped curtain wall and steeped in Jacobite intrigue. Volunteer-run and wonderfully intimate.
history: Built in 1628 by John Erskine, Earl of Mar, as a hunting lodge to counter the rising Farquharsons. Burned by the Black Colonel of Inverey during the 1689 Jacobite rising. After the 1745 uprising, Hanoverian troops garrisoned here, leaving 18th-century graffiti still visible in the dining room. Now owned by Clan Farquharson and run by local volunteers.

name: Old Wick Castle
description: A gaunt Norse tower clinging to a knife-edge promontory above the North Sea â€” one of Scotland's oldest castles and among its most dramatically sited. Known locally as 'The Old Man of Wick'.
history: Traditionally attributed to Harald Maddadson, Norse Jarl of Caithness and Orkney, in the 12th century, though the surviving four-storey tower may date to the 14th century. Reached only via a narrow land bridge with a rock-cut ditch once spanned by drawbridge. Parts of the seaward wall have collapsed into the sea. A scheduled monument managed by Historic Environment Scotland.

name: Castle Girnigoe
description: A spectacular ruined stronghold of Clan Sinclair perched on a jagged sea stack north of Wick. Twin castles â€” 15th-century Girnigoe and 17th-century Sinclair â€” fused into one brooding coastal ruin.
history: Built by William Sinclair, 2nd Earl of Caithness, between 1476 and 1496 on a dramatic promontory above Sinclair Bay. The 4th Earl infamously imprisoned and starved his own son here in 1577. Commandeered by Parliamentarian troops during the Civil War, rendered uninhabitable by the 1680s. Transferred to the Campbells of Glenorchy in 1672 to settle debts. Now under restoration by the Sinclair Castle Trust.

name: Sween Castle
description: Arguably the oldest stone castle on the Scottish mainland, brooding over Loch Sween in remote Knapdale. A Norman-style fortress with roots in the age of Norse sea-kings.
history: Thought to date from the late 11th or early 12th century, built by Suibhne 'the Red', a Norse-Gaelic lord. One of the earliest stone castles in Scotland, predating most Highland fortifications. Later held by the MacDonald Lords of the Isles. Besieged and partly destroyed by Robert the Bruce's forces in 1315. Remains include a rectangular keep with later 13th-century tower additions.

name: Dunyvaig Castle
description: Crumbling stronghold of the MacDonald Lords of the Isles, perched above Lagavulin Bay on Islay's whisky coast. Sea-salt ruins with a view straight into Scotland's clan warfare past.
history: A key naval fortress of the MacDonald Lords of the Isles from the 13th century, controlling the sea approaches to Islay. Besieged repeatedly during the 16th-17th century struggles between the MacDonalds and the Campbells. Fell to a Crown expedition in 1615 after the MacDonalds' final rebellion. Archaeological excavations have revealed rich material culture including Continental trade goods.

name: Finlaggan Castle
description: The atmospheric island seat of the Lords of the Isles, set on two crannogs in a remote Islay loch. Scotland's forgotten medieval capital â€” where a Gaelic parliament once ruled the western seas.
history: From the 12th to 15th century, Finlaggan was the ceremonial and administrative centre of the Lordship of the Isles. The MacDonald lords held council on Eilean MÃ²r and were inaugurated on the sacred Eilean na Comhairle (Council Island). The lordship was forfeited to the Scottish Crown in 1493. Archaeological excavations since the 1990s have revealed a complex of great halls, chapels, and workshops.

name: Dudhope Castle
description: A brooding 13th-century stronghold turned urban landmark in the heart of Dundee. Once the seat of the Scrymgeour hereditary standard-bearers, now wrapped in parkland above the Tay.
history: Originally a 13th-century fortification, rebuilt as a tower house by the Scrymgeour family who held the hereditary office of Royal Standard Bearer. John Graham of Claverhouse â€” 'Bonnie Dundee' â€” held the castle before his fateful Jacobite campaign of 1689. Served as a barracks, wool mill, and was nearly demolished before restoration. Set in Dudhope Park with views over the Firth of Tay.

name: Brechin Castle
description: A grand baronial pile overlooking the South Esk river, seat of the Ramsay Earls of Dalhousie. Ancient roots masked by centuries of elegant remodelling in warm Angus sandstone.
history: Site of a royal castle from at least the 13th century â€” besieged by Edward I in 1296 and again in 1303 during the Wars of Independence. Passed to the Maule family and then to the Ramsays, Earls of Dalhousie. Extensively remodelled in the 18th and 19th centuries. The grounds host the Brechin Castle Centre, a popular garden and retail attraction.

name: Lauriston Castle
description: An Edwardian time capsule on Edinburgh's Cramond shore â€” a 16th-century tower house wrapped in Jacobean extensions, filled with antiques and overlooking the Firth of Forth.
history: The original tower house was built around 1590 by Sir Archibald Napier, father of John Napier who invented logarithms. Extended in Jacobean style by Thomas Allan in the 1820s. Last private owners William and Margaret Reid bequeathed it to the city of Edinburgh in 1926, stipulating nothing be changed. The interiors remain an Edwardian snapshot with Crossley mosaics, Derbyshire Blue John, and fine furniture.

name: Dunbar Castle
description: Storm-battered ruins fused with volcanic rock on Dunbar's harbour front. Once one of Scotland's mightiest coastal fortresses, now a dramatic skeleton clawed by the North Sea.
history: A royal stronghold from at least the 11th century. 'Black Agnes', Countess of Dunbar, famously defended it against an English siege in 1338. Mary Queen of Scots fled here with Bothwell in 1567 after the murder of Rizzio. Demolished by Parliament in 1568 to prevent further use. The ruins were later incorporated into the harbour â€” cannon loops peer out between fishing boats.

name: Thirlestane Castle
description: One of Scotland's grandest inhabited castles, its fairy-tale turrets and extraordinary Restoration plasterwork ceilings rival any stately home. The Maitland family seat for over 400 years.
history: Built in the 16th century on a site dating to the 12th century near the Leader Water. Transformed by John Maitland, Duke of Lauderdale — Charles II's powerful Secretary of State — who employed Sir William Bruce to create palatial baroque interiors in the 1670s. The plasterwork ceilings by George Dunsterfield are among the finest in Europe. Still home to the Maitland family.

name: Fast Castle
description: A near-impossible clifftop ruin on the Berwickshire coast, accessible only by a vertiginous path. Sir Walter Scott's inspiration for Wolf's Crag in 'The Bride of Lammermoor'.
history: A 14th-century stronghold perched on a sheer promontory above the North Sea, held by the Home family. Captured and recaptured during the Anglo-Scottish wars and the 'Rough Wooing' of the 1540s. Allegedly linked to the Gowrie Conspiracy of 1600 and rumoured to hide treasure. The approach requires nerve — a narrow path across a chasm once spanned by drawbridge.

name: Castle of Old Wick
description: A gaunt Norse tower clinging to a knife-edge promontory above the North Sea — one of Scotland's oldest castles. Known locally as 'The Old Man of Wick'.
history: Traditionally attributed to Harald Maddadson, Norse Jarl of Caithness and Orkney, in the 12th century, though surviving structure may date to the 14th century. A four-storey tower reached via narrow land bridge with rock-cut ditch. Parts of the seaward wall have collapsed. Managed by Historic Environment Scotland as a scheduled monument.

name: Cardoness Castle
description: A remarkably complete 15th-century tower house standing tall above the Water of Fleet, with sweeping views to Wigtown Bay. A textbook example of late medieval lordly living.
history: Built around 1460 by the McCullochs of Galloway, a notoriously violent family. The six-storey tower retains its original stone benches, fireplace, and a gruesome murder-hole above the entrance. The McCullochs were eventually outlawed for their feuding. Passed to the Gordons and later to Historic Scotland. Fine views over Fleet Bay to the Machars.

name: Orchardton Tower
description: Scotland's only circular tower house — a unique architectural curiosity tucked into the rolling Galloway farmland. Charmingly small and perfectly formed.
history: Built in the mid-15th century by John Cairns, this is the only cylindrical tower house in Scotland. Standing about 33 feet tall with a spiral staircase, it originally had attached rectangular buildings now reduced to foundations. The tower passed through several families before coming into state care. A scheduled monument managed by Historic Environment Scotland.

name: Carsluith Castle
description: A compact L-plan tower house in a quiet corner of Galloway, built by the Browns who were mixed up in every local feud going. Peaceful now, with views over Wigtown Bay.
history: Built in the late 15th century by the Cairns family and acquired by the Browns of Carsluith around 1500. Richard Brown of Carsluith was implicated in the murder of the Abbot of Sweetheart Abbey in 1560. Extended with a stair tower in the 16th century. The castle was abandoned by the late 17th century. Now a well-preserved ruin in Historic Scotland care.

name: Hawick Motte
description: A great green mound in the heart of Hawick — one of the best-preserved Norman mottes in Scotland. A grassy hillock hiding centuries of Borders warfare beneath its turf.
history: A 12th-century Norman motte-and-bailey castle, likely built by the Lovels who held the barony of Hawick. The timber castle was replaced by stone structures which have since vanished, leaving the impressive earthwork. Located in the town centre near the River Teviot. The mound stands about 30 feet high and offers views over this historic Borders textile town.

name: Ferniehirst Castle
description: The ancestral seat of the Kerr clan, tucked into Jed Water valley near Jedburgh. A fine 16th-century tower house with famously left-handed staircases built for sinister swordsmen.
history: Originally built in 1470 by Sir Thomas Kerr. Destroyed by the English in 1523 and rebuilt. Recaptured from English occupation in 1549 with French assistance. The castle's anti-clockwise spiral staircases were designed for the left-handed Kerrs to fight more effectively. Now the seat of the Marquess of Lothian and open for limited tours. Houses a Borders heritage museum.

name: Greenknowe Tower
description: A handsome L-plan tower house standing in emerald farmland near Gordon in the Scottish Borders. Remarkably intact, with crow-stepped gables and fine carved details.
history: Built in 1581 by James Seton of Touch, as recorded on the marriage stone above the door. A well-preserved example of a Scottish laird's fortified home. Features include gun loops, a vaulted ground floor, and elegant Renaissance details. Passed to the Pringles of Stichill in the 17th century. Now in Historic Scotland care. Free to visit year-round.

name: Drochil Castle
description: A massive unfinished Elizabethan-era mansion in the Borders, planned as a palace but never completed. Scotland's great architectural 'what if', abandoned when its builder fell from power.
history: Begun around 1578 by Regent Morton (James Douglas, 4th Earl of Morton), intended as a grand Renaissance residence. Morton was executed in 1581 before it was completed. The Z-plan castle is remarkably large — more mansion than fortress — with traces of plaster and decorative work. One of the earliest attempts at a Scottish country house. Now a roofless but impressive shell on private land.

name: Dunoon Castle
description: Sparse remains of a royal castle commanding the Firth of Clyde from Castle Hill above Dunoon. Once a key stronghold controlling access to the western Highlands.
history: A 12th-century royal castle held by various Scottish kings and granted to the Campbells in the 14th century. The hereditary keeper was the chief of Clan Lamont until a notorious massacre in 1646 when Campbells killed hundreds of Lamonts here. Little survives above ground but the hilltop site offers panoramic views over the Clyde. Castle Hill gardens surround the remains.

name: Castle Lachlan
description: Romantic ruins of the ancient Maclachlan clan seat, standing on the shores of Loch Fyne amid wild Argyll scenery. A Georgian replacement nearby keeps the clan flame alive.
history: The original 13th-century castle was the seat of the Maclachlan chiefs for five centuries. Destroyed by government ships during the 1745 Jacobite rising — the chief was killed at Culloden. The ruins stand on a promontory jutting into Loch Fyne. A new Castle Lachlan was built nearby in 1790 and remains the clan seat. The old castle ruins are atmospheric and accessible.

name: Innis Chonnel Castle
description: A remote island fortress on Loch Awe — the ancient Campbell stronghold from which one of Scotland's most powerful clans launched their rise to dominance.
history: Built in the 13th century on an island in Loch Awe, this was the original seat of the Campbell chiefs before they moved to Inveraray. A strategic position controlling the loch, it served as the clan's power base throughout the medieval period. The ruins include a curtain wall castle with later tower additions. Accessible only by boat, adding to its wild remoteness.

name: Aros Castle
description: Gaunt ruins of a MacDougall stronghold on the Sound of Mull, once commanding the sea lanes between Mull and the mainland. Windswept and wild.
history: Built in the 13th century by the MacDougall lords of Lorn, later passing to the MacDonalds, Lords of the Isles. Strategically positioned to control shipping through the Sound of Mull. Used as a gathering point for the Lords of the Isles' fleet. Abandoned by the 17th century. The ruins stand on a promontory with sweeping views and are reached by a coastal walk from Salen.

name: Moy Castle
description: A stark 15th-century tower house guarding Lochbuie bay on Mull's wild southern coast. The MacLaine stronghold, complete with a legendary drowning pit in its dungeon.
history: Built in the 15th century by the MacLaine chiefs of Lochbuie. The tower stands three storeys high with a notorious bottle dungeon — a pit prison from which there was no escape. The castle controlled access to Lochbuie's natural harbour. Abandoned when the family built a new house nearby in the 18th century. The surrounding landscape is stunningly remote with standing stones and a beautiful bay.

name: Claig Castle
description: Ruined fortress on a tiny islet off Jura, controlling the Sound of Islay. A remote sentinel from the age of the Lords of the Isles, barely visited and wonderfully wild.
history: A 13th-century castle on Am Fraoch Eilean, a small island in the Sound of Islay between Jura and Islay. Built to control the strategic sea passage, likely by the MacDonald Lords of the Isles. Very little documented history survives. The fragmentary ruins are accessible only by boat, making this one of Scotland's most remote and unvisited castle sites.

name: Toward Castle
description: Ruined Lamont stronghold on the Cowal Peninsula, haunted by the memory of the 1646 Dunoon Massacre. A melancholy ruin with dark clan history.
history: Built in the 15th century as a seat of Clan Lamont. In 1646, during the Civil War, Campbells besieged the castle and treacherously massacred up to 200 Lamont men, women and children — bodies were dumped in mass graves in Dunoon. The castle was destroyed and never rebuilt. The ruins stand near Toward Point on the Cowal Peninsula with views across the Firth of Clyde.

name: Castle Moil
description: A romantic ruin crowning the harbour at Kyleakin on Skye, said to have been built by a Norse princess who stretched a chain across the strait to levy tolls on passing ships.
history: Also known as Caisteal Maol, this ruined keep overlooks the narrows between Skye and the mainland at Kyleakin. Legend attributes it to 'Saucy Mary', a Norwegian princess who taxed ships passing through the strait. More likely built by the MacKinnon clan in the 15th century. The tower stood guard until the clan moved their seat. Now a picturesque ruin visible from the Skye Bridge.

name: Duntulm Castle
description: Dramatic clifftop ruins on Skye's northernmost tip, overlooking the Minch towards the Outer Hebrides. The MacDonald seat on Skye, abandoned after a nursemaid dropped the chief's baby from a window.
history: Built on a dramatic coastal cliff in Trotternish, likely on the site of an earlier Norse fort. Seat of the MacDonald chiefs of Sleat from the early 15th century. According to legend, abandoned around 1730 after a nursemaid accidentally dropped the chief's infant heir from a window onto the rocks below. Views extend to the Outer Hebrides on clear days. A short walk from the road.

name: Tioram Castle Loch
description: One of Scotland's most photogenic castles, rising from a tidal island in Loch Moidart. The Clanranald MacDonalds' ancient seat, accessible on foot only at low tide.
history: Built in the 13th century on the tidal island of Eilean Tioram in Loch Moidart. Seat of the MacDonald Captains of Clanranald for centuries. The chief allegedly ordered it burned before departing for the 1715 Jacobite rising, to prevent government occupation. The dramatic ruins remain inaccessible at high tide, surrounded by white sand beaches and ancient oakwoods.

name: Lochinch Castle
description: A 19th-century baronial mansion set in the magnificent Castle Kennedy Gardens, between two lochs in Galloway. The Stair family seat, surrounded by some of Scotland's finest landscaped grounds.
history: Built in 1864-68 to replace the ruined Castle Kennedy (destroyed by fire in 1716) as the seat of the Earl of Stair. Designed by architect Robert Wardrop in Scottish baronial style. The real draw is the 75-acre Castle Kennedy Gardens, laid out in the early 18th century by Field Marshal Lord Stair using soldiers from the Royal Scots Greys and Inniskilling Fusiliers.

name: Tullibardine Chapel
description: One of Scotland's most complete medieval collegiate churches, hidden in rural Perthshire. A rare survival — virtually unaltered since the Reformation.
history: Founded in 1446 by Sir David Murray as a collegiate church. Remarkably, it escaped the destruction of the Reformation and survives almost intact, making it one of the most complete medieval churches in Scotland. Simple and austere with a pointed barrel vault. The Murray family became the Dukes of Atholl. Now in Historic Scotland care.

name: Burleigh Castle
description: A compact 15th-century tower house near Milnathort in Kinross-shire, with an unusual corner tower and rooftop caphouse. Quietly impressive Lowland Scottish fortification.
history: Built around 1500 by the Balfour family, Lords Balfour of Burleigh. The tower features a distinctive round corner tower with a square caphouse. Robert Balfour, 5th Lord, was convicted of murder in 1709 for shooting his former sweetheart's husband. He escaped from Edinburgh Castle disguised as a woman. The castle ruins are well-preserved and freely accessible.

name: Methven Castle
description: A substantial 17th-century castle in Perthshire, site of the Battle of Methven where Bruce was defeated. Now a private residence in rolling agricultural land.
history: The present castle dates from 1664, built by Patrick Smythe on a site with deep historical roots. In 1306, the Battle of Methven saw Robert the Bruce's forces routed by the English under Aymer de Valence. Margaret Tudor, widow of James IV, held the earlier castle as part of her dower lands. The current building is a fine example of Restoration-period architecture. Private, visible from the road.

name: Affleck Castle
description: One of the finest surviving tower houses in Angus — a remarkably complete 15th-century L-plan castle with a private chapel featuring a delicate sacrament house.
history: Built around 1475 by the Auchinleck family (anglicised to Affleck). The tower stands four storeys high with a solar chapel containing a rare sacrament house with carved tracery. Features include a vaulted great hall, parapet walk, and original iron yett. One of the best-preserved examples of a Scottish laird's tower. Now in Historic Scotland care with limited opening.

name: Findlater Castle
description: Vertiginous ruins clinging to a cliff edge above the Moray Firth — one of Scotland's most spectacularly sited castles, requiring a nerve-testing scramble to reach.
history: Perched on a precipitous sea cliff between Cullen and Sandend, Findlater dates from the 13th century. Held by the Ogilvie family who became Earls of Findlater. The castle was virtually impregnable due to its position, accessible only from one side via a steep path. Abandoned after the Ogilvies built Cullen House in the 17th century. The cliff-face approach is not for the faint-hearted.

name: Dunideer Castle
description: Enigmatic hilltop ruins crowning a prominent Aberdeenshire hill, with traces of both an Iron Age vitrified fort and a medieval castle. Ancient layers in a dramatic landscape.
history: The hilltop site features the remains of an Iron Age vitrified fort fused with a 13th-century castle, making it one of Scotland's most layered archaeological sites. The medieval castle, possibly built by the Earls of Mar, incorporated the earlier fortification. The prominent hill is visible for miles across the Garioch. David, Earl of Huntingdon, may have built the stone castle around 1260.

name: Lickleyhead Castle
description: A well-preserved Z-plan tower house in the Aberdeenshire countryside, recently restored as a holiday let. A handsome example of the Scottish baronial style.
history: Built around 1629 by the Forbes family, with possible earlier origins in the 16th century. A classic Z-plan tower house with round towers at opposing corners. The castle fell into disrepair but was restored in the 20th century. Now operates as a holiday accommodation. Features crow-stepped gables, gun loops, and original architectural details typical of northeast Scotland.

name: Glenbuchat Castle
description: A haunting Z-plan tower house in lonely upper Donside, built by the Gordons and last held by 'Old Glenbucket' — the legendary Jacobite who marched to war aged seventy.
history: Built in 1590 by John Gordon and his wife Helen Carnegie, as recorded on a marriage stone. A fine Z-plan tower house with round towers at diagonally opposite corners. Most famous for its last laird, John Gordon of Glenbuchat ('Old Glenbucket'), who joined the 1745 Jacobite rising aged 70. The name supposedly terrified George II. Now a roofless ruin in Historic Scotland care.

name: Dufftown Clock Tower
description: The iconic clock tower standing sentinel at the crossroads of the 'Malt Whisky Capital of the World'. Originally a burgh jail, now Dufftown's defining landmark.
history: Built in 1839 by the Earl of Fife as the town's main civic building, originally housing the burgh jail. The tower stands at the junction of four streets in the centre of Dufftown, which is home to several major Speyside distilleries including Glenfiddich. The clock was added later. Now serves as a visitor information point and local heritage centre.

name: Lochindorb Castle
description: A brooding island fortress in a lonely Highland loch, once the lair of the 'Wolf of Badenoch' — Scotland's most notorious medieval warlord. Accessible only by boat.
history: Built in the 13th century on an island in Lochindorb, a bleak moorland loch south of Nairn. Most infamously held by Alexander Stewart, Earl of Buchan — the 'Wolf of Badenoch' — who terrorised the Highlands and burned Elgin Cathedral in 1390. Later used by Edward I of England as a base. The island castle is visible from the shore but only reachable by boat.

name: Rait Castle
description: A rare and eerie 13th-century hall house hidden in woodland near Nairn, scene of a treacherous clan massacre. One of Scotland's oldest surviving domestic buildings.
history: Built around 1300 by Sir Gervase de Rait, this is one of Scotland's oldest surviving unfortified hall houses. In 1442, the Comyns invited the Mackintoshes to a feast here and planned to murder them at a signal. The plot was betrayed, and the Comyns themselves were massacred instead. The roofless ruin retains fine window openings and a remarkable survival of medieval domestic architecture.

name: Dornoch Castle
description: A 15th-century bishop's palace turned charming hotel in the heart of Dornoch, just across from the magnificent cathedral. Gothic grandeur meets Highland hospitality.
history: Originally built in the 15th century as the palace of the Bishops of Caithness, adjacent to Dornoch Cathedral. Damaged during clan feuds between the Murrays and Mackays. Rebuilt and extended over the centuries. Converted into a hotel in the 20th century — now the Dornoch Castle Hotel. The town is famous for its cathedral, golf links, and being the last place in Scotland where a witch was burned (1727).

name: Castle Sinclair Girnigoe
description: A spectacular ruined stronghold of Clan Sinclair perched on a jagged sea stack north of Wick. Twin castles fused into one brooding coastal ruin with centuries of dark history.
history: Built by William Sinclair, 2nd Earl of Caithness, between 1476 and 1496 on a dramatic promontory above Sinclair Bay. The 4th Earl imprisoned his own son here in 1577, who starved to death. Commandeered by Parliamentarian troops during the Civil War, uninhabitable by the 1680s. Now under restoration by the Sinclair Castle Trust.

name: Bucholie Castle
description: Fragmentary Norse-era ruins on a dramatic sea stack near Freswick in Caithness. Linked to the legendary Sweyn Asleifsson — the 'Ultimate Viking'.
history: Perched on a sea stack on the Caithness coast near Freswick Bay, traditionally identified as the stronghold of Sweyn Asleifsson, described in the Orkneyinga Saga as the last great Viking warrior. The ruins are fragmentary and the site is dangerously exposed. Dates to the 12th century Norse period. One of the most atmospheric and least-visited castle sites in Scotland.

name: Ackergill Tower
description: A striking 15th-century tower house on the Caithness coast, now a luxury hotel. Centuries of Keith-Gunn clan feuds echo through its well-restored walls.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Keith clan, hereditary Earls Marischal of Scotland. The tower was the focus of fierce feuding with the Gunns clan — in one incident, the Keiths kidnapped Helen Gunn, 'the Beauty of Braemore', who threw herself from the tower rather than submit. Extensively restored in the 19th century. Now operates as an exclusive-use hotel and events venue.

name: Castle of Park
description: A handsome 16th-century tower house in Galloway, built by the son of the last Abbot of Glenluce who acquired church lands at the Reformation. Reformed faith, unreformed ambition.
history: Built around 1590 by Thomas Hay, son of the last Abbot of Glenluce Abbey, using stone from the abbey itself. A well-preserved four-storey tower house with bartizans and crow-stepped gables. The Hays profited handsomely from the Reformation, converting monastic wealth into secular power. Restored in the 20th century and now available as holiday accommodation.

name: Comlongon Castle
description: A massive 15th-century tower house in Dumfriesshire, said to be haunted by a green lady — a Murray bride who threw herself from the battlements. Now a wedding venue and hotel.
history: Built around 1450 by the Murray family. The tower is one of the largest in southern Scotland, standing 50 feet high with walls up to 14 feet thick. Lady Marion Carruthers allegedly leapt from the castle in 1570 rather than submit to a forced marriage. Her ghost — the Green Lady — is said to haunt the grounds. A Victorian mansion was attached in the 19th century. Now a popular wedding venue and hotel.

name: Barra Castle
description: A modest but charming tower house near Old Meldrum in Aberdeenshire, now beautifully restored as a family home. Quiet baronial dignity amid fertile farmland.
history: Dating from the 16th century, built by the King family. A compact tower house later extended with a wing. Fell into ruin before being restored in the 20th century. Located near Old Meldrum in the heart of Aberdeenshire's agricultural lowlands. Not to be confused with Kisimul Castle on the Isle of Barra. Private residence, visible from the road.

name: Pitfichie Castle
description: A restored 15th-century tower house in rural Aberdeenshire, rescued from near-total ruin and rebuilt as a private home. A phoenix of Scottish baronial architecture.
history: Originally built in the 15th century, possibly by the Hurry family, later passing to various Aberdeenshire lairds. The castle fell into severe ruin with only fragments remaining. Painstakingly restored in the late 20th century using traditional methods and materials. The restoration is considered one of the finest examples of tower house reconstruction in Scotland. Private residence.

name: Cluny Castle
description: A Scottish baronial mansion in Aberdeenshire, rebuilt after a dramatic fire. The Gordon seat, set in extensive grounds near the village of Cluny.
history: The original castle dated from the medieval period, held by the Gordon family. Rebuilt in the 18th century and again after a fire in 1787. The current building incorporates elements from various periods. Set in the Aberdeenshire countryside near Sauchen. The estate has been associated with the Gordons of Cluny for centuries. Private residence.

name: Leslie Castle
description: A beautifully restored 17th-century tower house in Aberdeenshire, rescued from ruin and now a holiday venue. Baronial elegance brought back from the brink.
history: Built in 1661 by William Forbes, replacing an earlier castle of the Leslie family which dated from the 12th century. A fine example of a Scottish baronial tower house with turrets and crow-stepped gables. Fell into ruin and was rescued by David Leslie, who painstakingly restored it. Now available as a holiday let and events venue.

name: Fetteresso Castle
description: A ruined baronial mansion near Stonehaven, once a Keith stronghold and Episcopalian chapel during the penal era. Dignified decay in the Kincardineshire woodlands.
history: Site has medieval origins as a stronghold of the Keith Earls Marischal. The surviving building dates mainly from the 17th century. After the Keiths were forfeited following the 1715 Jacobite rising, the castle served as an Episcopalian chapel when such worship was outlawed. Later used as a farmstead. The ruins are substantial and atmospheric, set in woodland near Stonehaven.

name: Inverugie Castle
description: Overgrown ruins of a Keith stronghold near Peterhead, slowly being reclaimed by nature. A once-grand residence of the Earls Marischal now wrapped in ivy and silence.
history: Originally a medieval castle of the Cheyne family, passing to the Keith Earls Marischal in the 14th century. Rebuilt in the 16th-17th centuries as a substantial residence. Forfeited after the 1715 Jacobite rising when the 10th Earl Marischal supported the Old Pretender. The ruins are extensive but overgrown, set near the River Ugie outside Peterhead.

name: Pitsligo Castle
description: Substantial ruins of a Forbes stronghold in Buchan, with a rare medieval great hall. The last Lord Pitsligo's Jacobite loyalty cost his family everything.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Forbes family, later Lords Pitsligo. Features a rare surviving 15th-century great hall with pointed-arch windows. Alexander Forbes, 4th Lord Pitsligo, joined the 1745 Jacobite rising aged 67 and spent years as a fugitive, hidden by loyal tenants. The estate was forfeited and the castle fell into ruin. Now accessible as a free monument.

name: Auchindoir Castle
description: Fragmentary but evocative ruins in a remote Aberdeenshire glen, notable for the adjacent Romanesque church of St Mary. Ancient sacred ground in lonely hill country.
history: The castle dates from the 13th century, held by various families including the Gordons. Only fragments of curtain wall survive. The real treasure is the adjacent ruined church of St Mary of Auchindoir, which retains a fine Romanesque doorway and sacrament house — rare survivals in rural Aberdeenshire. The site is remote and atmospheric, in the upper Don valley.

name: Esslemont Castle
description: A crumbling tower house in woodland near Ellon, Aberdeenshire. Ivy-wrapped ruins with an air of romantic neglect — the kind of ruin poets dream about.
history: Built in the 13th century by the Cheyne family, passing to the Gordons and later the Forbeses. The main surviving structure is a 16th-century L-plan tower house. The castle was occupied until the 18th century before falling into ruin. Partly demolished for building materials. The atmospheric remains stand in woodland and are accessible via a short walk.

name: Cairnbulg Castle
description: A striking 13th-century fortress on the Aberdeenshire coast near Fraserburgh, restored and privately owned. One of the oldest continuously occupied castles in the region.
history: Originally built in the 13th century by the Comyn Earls of Buchan. Passed to the Frasers after the Comyns' downfall, then to the Gordons. The castle was extensively restored in the 19th and 20th centuries. Features include a massive round tower and later baronial additions. Set near the village of Cairnbulg on the northeast coast. Private residence.

name: Harthill Castle
description: A Z-plan tower house in the Aberdeenshire countryside, restored from ruin and now a private residence. A classic example of northeast Scotland's distinctive castle architecture.
history: Built around 1601 by the Leith family on the site of an earlier fortalice. A well-proportioned Z-plan castle with round towers at diagonally opposite corners. Fell into serious disrepair but was restored in the 20th century. The castle stands in farmland near Oyne in the Garioch district of Aberdeenshire. Private residence, visible from the road.

name: Craig Castle
description: A substantial L-plan tower house in rural Aberdeenshire, with medieval origins and later baronial additions. Quiet nobility in the Donside landscape.
history: Dating from the 16th century with possible earlier origins, held by the Gordon family. An L-plan tower house extended over the centuries. The castle stands near the village of Rhynie in Strathdon. Features include a vaulted ground floor, turret staircase, and later wing additions. Private residence set in the rolling Aberdeenshire countryside.

name: Midmar Castle
description: One of the finest Z-plan castles in Scotland, built by the legendary mason-architect family of Bell. A triumph of Aberdeenshire's golden age of castle building.
history: Built around 1575, attributed to the Bell family of master masons who also built Castle Fraser and Crathes Castle. A superb Z-plan tower house with round towers and a magnificent great hall. The design represents the peak of northeast Scottish castle architecture. Private residence on a wooded estate near Echt. One of several 'Castles of Mar' on the Castle Trail.

name: Towie Barclay Castle
description: A beautifully restored 16th-century tower house near Turriff, notable for its spectacular ribbed vaulted great hall — one of the finest domestic interiors in northeast Scotland.
history: Built in 1593 by the Barclay family, replacing an earlier castle on the site. The great hall features an extraordinary ribbed vault ceiling, unique in Scottish domestic architecture. The castle fell into ruin in the 19th century but was lovingly restored from 1970 by Marc Ellington. The restoration is considered one of the finest in Scotland. Private residence with occasional openings.

name: Gight Castle
description: Haunting ruins above the River Ythan in Aberdeenshire, once a Gordon stronghold and ancestral home of Lord Byron's mother. Abandoned, overgrown, and deliciously eerie.
history: Built in the 16th century by the Gordons of Gight, notorious for their wild and lawless ways. Catherine Gordon of Gight married 'Mad Jack' Byron in 1785 and sold the estate to pay his debts — their son was the poet Lord Byron. The castle was abandoned and the roof stripped. The ruins perch dramatically above a gorge on the River Ythan, reached by a woodland path.

name: Castle Menzies
description: A magnificent 16th-century Z-plan castle in Highland Perthshire, magnificently restored by the Menzies Clan Society. Bonnie Prince Charlie slept here — and his room is still shown.
history: Built in 1571 by the Menzies clan chief, replacing an earlier castle. Bonnie Prince Charlie stayed here in 1746 on his march north, followed days later by the Duke of Cumberland. The castle served as the Menzies seat for over 400 years before falling into disrepair. Rescued from ruin by the Menzies Clan Society in 1957 and painstakingly restored. Now a clan museum and visitor attraction.

name: Grandtully Castle
description: A tower house overlooking the Tay valley in Perthshire, notable for its nearby church with astonishing painted timber ceiling — a hidden masterpiece of Scottish art.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Stewart family, later extended. The castle itself is a private residence, but the adjacent St Mary's Church contains one of Scotland's greatest treasures — a painted timber ceiling from the early 17th century, depicting heraldic and religious scenes in vibrant colour. The castle and church sit in the beautiful Tay valley near Aberfeldy.

name: Megginch Castle
description: A 15th-century tower house in the Carse of Gowrie, home to the Drummonds for over 500 years. Famous for its ancient gardens, topiary, and a 1,000-year-old yew tree.
history: Built in the 15th century and continuously occupied by the Drummond family (later Cherry-Drummonds) since then. The castle has medieval origins with extensive later additions. The gardens are particularly notable, featuring ancient topiary, a physic garden, and what may be Scotland's oldest yew tree. Used as a filming location. Open for limited garden visits and events.

name: Balhousie Castle
description: A 15th-century castle in Perth, now home to the Black Watch Museum — showcasing 350 years of Scotland's most famous regiment. Military heritage in baronial splendour.
history: Originally a 15th-century tower house, extensively remodelled in the 19th century in Scottish baronial style. Home to various Perth families before being acquired by the Black Watch regiment. Now houses the Black Watch Castle and Museum, telling the story of this legendary Highland regiment from its founding in 1739 to the present day. Set in parkland on the edge of Perth's North Inch.

name: Balwearie Castle
description: A ruined tower house near Kirkcaldy in Fife, once the seat of the Scott family. Standing quietly in suburbia, a medieval survivor in an unlikely setting.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Scott family, later Scotts of Balwearie. Sir Michael Scott of Balwearie was a famous medieval scholar and reputed wizard. The tower stands about 40 feet high with a vaulted ground floor. Now surrounded by modern housing on the outskirts of Kirkcaldy. A scheduled monument, somewhat incongruously set amid suburban streets.

name: Newark Castle (Fife)
description: Ruined 15th-century castle on the Fife coast near St Monans, overlooking the Firth of Forth. Not to be confused with its better-known Port Glasgow namesake.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Sandilands family, later held by the Leslies and then the Bairds. A compact tower house on the coastal path between Elie and St Monans. David Leslie, who defeated Montrose at Philiphaugh in 1645, was associated with the castle. The ruins are modest but the coastal setting is delightful, part of the Fife Coastal Path.

name: Earl's Palace Birsay
description: Substantial Renaissance ruins overlooking the Brough of Birsay in Orkney. Built by the tyrannical Earl Robert Stewart — oppression made beautiful in red Orcadian sandstone.
history: Built around 1574 by Robert Stewart, Earl of Orkney, an illegitimate son of James V. Stewart was a notorious tyrant who used forced labour to construct his palaces. The building is an impressive example of Scottish Renaissance architecture with a courtyard plan. His son Patrick Stewart built the even grander Earl's Palace in Kirkwall. Both were eventually executed for treason.

name: Borve Castle (Benbecula)
description: A lonely ruin on the machair of Benbecula in the Outer Hebrides — a rare medieval tower house in a landscape of wind, sea, and sky.
history: Built in the 14th century, probably by the MacDonalds of Clanranald. One of the few medieval tower houses surviving in the Outer Hebrides. The castle is a simple rectangular structure standing to its full height on the western coast of Benbecula. Amy MacRuari, divorced wife of John of Islay, Lord of the Isles, is traditionally associated with the castle. Freely accessible.

name: Calvay Castle
description: A tiny ruined castle on an islet in Loch Boisdale, South Uist. Bonnie Prince Charlie sheltered here during his flight through the Outer Hebrides.
history: A small 15th-century castle on an island in Loch Boisdale, held by the MacNeils. Most famous as a refuge for Bonnie Prince Charlie in June 1746 during his flight after Culloden, before Flora MacDonald helped him escape to Skye. The ruins are fragmentary and only accessible by boat. One of the most remote castle sites in Scotland.

name: Caisteal Maol
description: A romantic ruin crowning the harbour at Kyleakin on Skye, said to have been built by a Norse princess who stretched a chain across the strait to levy tolls on passing ships.
history: Also known as Castle Moil, this ruined keep overlooks the narrows between Skye and the mainland at Kyleakin. Legend attributes it to 'Saucy Mary', a Norwegian princess who taxed ships. More likely built by the MacKinnon clan in the 15th century. The fragmentary tower is a picturesque ruin visible from the Skye Bridge. A short walk from the village.

name: Duns Castle
description: A baronial mansion set in a 1,200-acre nature reserve in the Borders town of Duns. Gothic Revival grandeur meets outstanding wildlife habitat.
history: The estate has medieval origins — a castle stood here from at least the 14th century. The current building dates from extensive remodelling in 1818-22 by architect James Gillespie Graham in Gothic Revival style. The grounds include Duns Castle Nature Reserve with ancient woodland, a man-made lake, and rich biodiversity. Now used as an events and wedding venue. The town of Duns was the birthplace of racing driver Jim Clark.

name: Duntrune Castle
description: The oldest continuously occupied castle on the Scottish mainland, standing guard over Loch Crinan in Argyll. A Campbell stronghold with a ghostly piper legend.
history: Dating from the 12th or 13th century, claimed to be the oldest continuously inhabited castle on the Scottish mainland. Originally a MacDougall fortress, it passed to the Campbells in the 13th century. Legend tells of a MacDonald piper who was imprisoned here and had his hands cut off for playing a warning to approaching clansmen. A fingerless skeleton was found beneath the floor in the 20th century. Private but visible from the road.

name: Fraoch Eilean Castle
description: A ruined island castle in Loch Awe, surrounded by ancient legend and accessible only by boat. One of several atmospheric strongholds dotting this vast Highland loch.
history: Built in the 13th century on an island in Loch Awe, one of several island castles on the loch. Associated with the MacDougalls and later the Campbells. The name means 'Heather Island' in Gaelic. Legend connects the island with the mythical hero Fraoch from Irish mythology. The fragmentary ruins are evocative, set against the backdrop of Ben Cruachan and the surrounding mountains.

name: Luffness Castle
description: A private castle in East Lothian with ancient origins, set near the famous Luffness golf links. Medieval fortress turned Georgian country house, still family-owned.
history: The site dates from at least the 12th century, with a Norse fort preceding the medieval castle. Held by the de Lindsay family, then the Hepburns, and later the Hopes. Besieged during the 'Rough Wooing' in the 1540s when a French garrison defended it. The medieval elements were incorporated into a later country house. Surrounded by the Luffness estate and adjacent to Aberlady Bay nature reserve.

name: Inverquharity Castle
description: A restored 15th-century tower house near Kirriemuir in Angus, rescued from ruin and beautifully rebuilt. A Bonnie Prince Charlie connection adds romantic lustre.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Ogilvy family. The castle was involved in the Jacobite risings and suffered accordingly. Fell into ruin before being restored in the mid-20th century. Features include a vaulted great hall and corbelled parapet. Set in the gentle Angus countryside near Kirriemuir, birthplace of J.M. Barrie. Private residence.

name: Inverallochy Castle
description: A ruined 16th-century tower near Cairnbulg on the Aberdeenshire coast. A compact Comyn stronghold, modest but atmospheric.
history: Built in the 16th century by the Comyn family on the northeast coast near Fraserburgh. A simple rectangular tower house, now roofless but with walls standing to a good height. Set in farmland near the fishing village of Inverallochy. The castle is freely accessible and offers views towards the North Sea. One of many tower houses that dot the Buchan coastline.

name: Kinnaird Head Castle
description: Scotland's first lighthouse, built atop a 16th-century castle tower at Fraserburgh. A unique fusion of medieval fortress and Stevenson engineering, now the Museum of Scottish Lighthouses.
history: The tower house was built in 1570 by Sir Alexander Fraser, founder of Fraserburgh. In 1787, the Northern Lighthouse Board converted it into Scotland's first mainland lighthouse, designed by Thomas Smith. Robert Stevenson later modified the light. Now houses the Museum of Scottish Lighthouses, telling the story of Scotland's vital lighthouse network. A scheduled ancient monument.

name: Udny Castle
description: A five-storey tower house near Udny Green in Aberdeenshire, restored and privately inhabited. Notable for being one of the tallest intact tower houses in the region.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Udny family who held the estate for centuries. A substantial five-storey tower with later additions. The castle was restored in the 19th and 20th centuries. The nearby Udny Green village features the famous 'Udny Mort House' — a watch house built to protect graves from body-snatchers. Private residence.

name: Balquhain Castle
description: Ruins of a Leslie stronghold near Inverurie, scene of a bloody battle where the laird's wife defended it by pouring boiling fat on attackers.
history: Built in the 14th century by the Leslie family, who held it for centuries. In 1526, during a siege by the Forbes clan, the lady of the castle allegedly poured boiling fat from the battlements onto the attackers. The castle was burned by Covenanters in 1639. Substantial ruins survive, including a tower and courtyard walls. Set in farmland near Inverurie.

name: Fedderate Castle
description: Fragmentary ruins of a Crawford stronghold in Buchan, surrounded by legends of buried treasure and underground passages.
history: Built in the 13th century, probably by the Crawfords. A motte-and-bailey castle later replaced by stone structures. Associated with legends of hidden treasure and secret tunnels. The ruins are modest — mainly earthworks and wall fragments — set in flat Buchan farmland near New Deer. Freely accessible as a scheduled monument.

name: Ardiffery Castle
description: Scant remains of a small tower house in rural Buchan, near Cruden Bay. One of many minor fortified houses that once dotted northeast Scotland.
history: A 16th-century tower house in the parish of Cruden, Aberdeenshire. Very little remains above ground. The site is significant as part of the dense network of minor lairdly residences that characterised medieval Buchan. Located in farmland, with minimal interpretation. Archaeological significance rather than visual spectacle.

name: Corse Castle
description: A substantial ruined tower house in Aberdeenshire, built by the Forbes family. An impressive shell with fine architectural details still visible.
history: Built around 1581 by William Forbes, as dated by an inscribed panel. A tall tower house with turrets and shot holes, typical of the Aberdeenshire castle-building tradition. The Forbes family were prominent in the region's complex web of clan rivalries. The castle was abandoned in the 18th century and is now a picturesque ruin. Freely accessible.

name: Hallforest Castle
description: A ruined 13th-century royal hunting lodge near Kintore in Aberdeenshire. Once a forest retreat for Scottish kings, now a quiet ruin in farmland.
history: Built in the 13th century as a royal hunting lodge associated with the Forest of Kintore. Granted to the Keith Earls Marischal by Robert the Bruce. A rectangular tower house, now ruined but with walls standing to a good height. Set in flat Aberdeenshire farmland near the town of Kintore. The surrounding forest has long since vanished.

name: Finavon Castle
description: Ruins of a medieval castle in Strathmore, Angus, perched above the South Esk river. Once seat of the Lindsay Earls of Crawford.
history: A castle has stood on this site since at least the 14th century, held by the Lindsay Earls of Crawford — known as the 'Tiger Earls' for their ferocity. The 'Tiger Earl' Alexander Lindsay, 4th Earl of Crawford, was one of the most powerful and violent nobles of 15th-century Scotland. The castle was later replaced by Finavon House. Nearby Finavon Hill has a vitrified fort.

name: Melgund Castle
description: A substantial L-plan tower house in Angus, built by Cardinal David Beaton — Scotland's most powerful and controversial churchman. Ruins of ecclesiastical ambition.
history: Built around 1543 by Cardinal David Beaton, Archbishop of St Andrews and effective ruler of Scotland, who was assassinated in 1546. A large L-plan tower house that was never completed due to the Cardinal's murder. The substantial ruins show what would have been a grand residence. Passed to the Gordon and then Elliot families. Freely accessible in farmland near Aberlemno.

name: Baikie Castle
description: Minimal remains of a medieval castle near Tankerness in Orkney. A Norse-Scottish stronghold reduced to earthworks in the Orcadian landscape.
history: A medieval castle site in the parish of Tankerness on Mainland Orkney. Very little survives above ground — mainly earthworks and wall foundations. Associated with the Norse-Scottish lords of Orkney. The surrounding landscape is typical flat Orcadian farmland. Of mainly archaeological interest.

name: Kinclaven Castle
description: Overgrown ruins of a royal castle above the confluence of the Tay and Isla in Perthshire. Edward I of England stayed here during the Wars of Independence.
history: A royal castle dating from at least the 12th century, strategically positioned above the meeting of the Rivers Tay and Isla. Edward I of England used it as a base during his Scottish campaigns. Later held by various Scottish noble families. The ruins are fragmentary and heavily overgrown, surrounded by woodland. The setting above the river confluence is dramatic.

name: Garth Castle
description: A well-preserved 14th-century tower house in Glen Lyon, Perthshire. Built by the notorious 'Wolf of Badenoch', now dramatically set against Highland scenery.
history: Built around 1400, traditionally by Alexander Stewart, the 'Wolf of Badenoch' — the same warlord who burned Elgin Cathedral. A four-storey tower house with later additions, standing in the beautiful upper reaches of Glen Lyon. The castle was restored in the 20th century and is now a private residence. The glen setting with mountains on all sides is magnificent.

name: Newton Castle (Perth)
description: A 16th-century tower house near Blairgowrie in Perthshire, restored and privately owned. Compact baronial elegance in the berry-growing heartland.
history: Built in the 16th century, likely by the Drummond family. A compact tower house with turrets and corbelling, typical of the period. Set near Blairgowrie in the fertile Strathmore region. Restored in the 20th century and now a private residence. The area is famous for its raspberry and strawberry farms.

name: Clunie Castle
description: Traces of a once-important island castle in the Loch of Clunie, near Blairgowrie. A medieval bishops' residence now reduced to fragments on a crannog.
history: Located on an island (or crannog) in the Loch of Clunie, this was a residence of the Bishops of Dunkeld from the medieval period. The castle saw action during the Wars of Independence and was used by various Scottish kings. Very little survives above the waterline. The loch is peaceful and the site is mainly of archaeological interest.

name: Stobhall Castle
description: A remarkable cluster of medieval buildings on a cliff above the River Tay — the ancient Drummond seat, with a painted chapel ceiling that ranks among Scotland's greatest art treasures.
history: A complex of buildings dating from the 14th century onwards, the ancestral seat of the Drummond family (later Earls of Perth). Perched dramatically on cliffs above the Tay near Stanley. The chapel contains a stunning 16th-century painted ceiling with biblical and allegorical scenes. One of the most important vernacular medieval sites in Scotland. Private but occasionally open for guided visits.

name: Finlarig Castle
description: Ruined stronghold of 'Black Duncan' Campbell of Glenorchy at Killin, with a sinister beheading pit. Dark Highland justice at the gateway to the Breadalbane empire.
history: Built around 1629 by Sir Duncan Campbell of Glenorchy ('Black Duncan'), who founded the vast Breadalbane estate. The castle features a notorious beheading pit — nobles were decapitated here, while commoners were hanged from a nearby tree. The ruins stand near the western end of Loch Tay at Killin. A chilling reminder of the absolute power wielded by Highland chiefs.

name: Fatlips Castle
description: A restored Border peel tower perched on the summit of Minto Crags, offering sweeping views over the Teviot valley. A perfect little fortress in a perfect Border setting.
history: A 16th-century peel tower built by the Turnbull family on the dramatic summit of Minto Crags in the Scottish Borders. The name 'Fatlips' may derive from a Turnbull chief. Restored by the Elliot family in the 19th century and again in the 20th. Reached by a steep walk through woodland. The views from the top over Teviotdale are outstanding.

name: Littledean Tower
description: A fine Border peel tower near Maxton, standing amid the gentle rolling landscape of Roxburghshire. A well-preserved example of the Borders' defensive architecture.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Kerr family, one of the powerful Border reiving clans. A rectangular tower house with later additions. The tower is well-preserved with a vaulted ground floor and original features. Set in the agricultural landscape near the River Tweed. Passed through various hands and is now privately owned. Visible from the road.

name: Hume Castle
description: A prominent Border hilltop fortress with panoramic views over the Merse, rebuilt as an 18th-century folly on medieval foundations. The Home clan's ancient seat, visible for miles.
history: An ancient stronghold of the Home family (Earls of Home) dating from at least the 13th century. Besieged and captured repeatedly during Anglo-Scottish conflicts. The garrison's defiant response 'I, Willie Wastle, stand firm in my castle' became a famous nursery rhyme. The medieval castle was destroyed and rebuilt as a castellated folly in 1794. The hilltop views across the Borders to the Cheviots are magnificent.

name: Roxburgh Castle
description: Earthwork remains of one of medieval Scotland's most important royal castles, where James II was killed by an exploding cannon. A pivotal site reduced to grassy mounds.
history: One of the four great royal burghs of medieval Scotland, Roxburgh Castle controlled a vital crossing of the Tweed. Repeatedly fought over during the Wars of Independence. In 1460, King James II was killed here when a cannon exploded beside him during the siege to recapture it from the English. His queen urged the army on, and the castle was taken and demolished. Only earthworks survive.

name: Edrom Castle
description: Minimal traces of a medieval castle in the village of Edrom, Berwickshire. More significant as an archaeological site than a visual spectacle.
history: A medieval castle site in the village of Edrom in the Scottish Borders. Very little remains above ground. The parish of Edrom was historically significant, with a Norman church (parts of which survive) and connections to various Border families. The castle site is of mainly archaeological interest.

name: Elibank Castle
description: A ruined tower house above the Tweed in a secluded Borders valley, associated with the legend of 'Muckle Mou'd Meg' — a famously ugly bride given as punishment.
history: A 16th-century tower house of the Murray family above the River Tweed. According to legend, a young Scott of Harden was caught stealing Murray cattle and given the choice of hanging or marrying Murray's plain-faced daughter Meg — he chose Meg, and the marriage proved happy. The ruins are atmospheric, hidden in woodland above the Tweed between Innerleithen and Galashiels.

name: Drumlanrig Castle
description: A magnificent pink sandstone palace in upper Nithsdale — the 'Pink Palace' of the Dukes of Buccleuch. Renaissance grandeur in a vast Borders estate with a world-class art collection.
history: Built 1679-91 for William Douglas, 1st Duke of Queensberry, who reportedly spent one night there and declared it too expensive to live in. A masterpiece of late 17th-century Renaissance architecture in pink sandstone. Houses an important art collection including a Leonardo da Vinci. In 2003, a Da Vinci painting was stolen and recovered. Set in 90,000 acres of Buccleuch estates with walking, cycling, and nature trails.

name: Barholm Castle
description: A beautifully restored 16th-century tower house in the Galloway Hills, home of John Knox's protector. Baronial dignity in a gentle green landscape.
history: Built around 1560 by the McCullochs. John Knox, the Scottish Reformation leader, is believed to have sheltered here with John McCulloch before fleeing to the Continent. A compact tower house with turrets and crow-stepped gables. Fell into ruin but was superbly restored in the early 2000s and is now a holiday let. Set in the beautiful Galloway countryside near Creetown.

name: Kirkcudbright Castle
description: Scant remains of a once-important castle in the artists' town of Kirkcudbright. The MacLellans later built their magnificent townhouse nearby, which still stands.
history: A medieval castle stood on this site controlling the harbour town of Kirkcudbright. The original castle was of some importance but was largely demolished. The MacLellans built MacLellan's Castle (a grand Renaissance townhouse) nearby in the 1570s using stones from the Greyfriars monastery. Kirkcudbright itself became famous as an artists' colony in the early 20th century.

name: Sanquhar Castle
description: Brooding ruins above the River Nith in upper Dumfriesshire. A Crichton stronghold associated with two famous declarations posted on the town cross.
history: Built in the 13th century by the Crichton family, later passing to the Douglases. The castle is associated with the Sanquhar Declarations of 1680 and 1685, when Covenanters publicly renounced allegiance to the Stuart kings. The ruins stand on a promontory above the Nith. William Crichton, Chancellor of Scotland, held the castle in the 15th century. Now a scheduled monument.

name: New Abbey Corn Mill
description: A beautifully preserved 18th-century water-powered corn mill in the village of New Abbey, near the haunting ruins of Sweetheart Abbey. Working heritage brought to life.
history: An 18th-century water-powered oatmeal mill, fully restored to working order by Historic Scotland. Set in the picturesque village of New Abbey alongside the romantic ruins of Sweetheart Abbey (founded 1273 by Lady Devorgilla in memory of her husband). The mill demonstrates traditional milling techniques and is one of the best-preserved water mills in Scotland.

name: Jarlshof
description: One of Europe's most remarkable archaeological sites — 4,000 years of continuous habitation from the Bronze Age to the Viking era, exposed on a Shetland clifftop. Time made visible.
history: Occupied continuously from around 2500 BC to the 17th century AD, Jarlshof reveals Bronze Age houses, Iron Age brochs and wheelhouses, Pictish structures, a Viking longhouse settlement, and a medieval farmstead all in one extraordinary site. Named by Sir Walter Scott (who invented the word). Located at Sumburgh Head on the southern tip of Shetland. Managed by Historic Environment Scotland.

name: Clickimin Broch
description: A remarkable Iron Age broch and settlement on a small island in a loch on the outskirts of Lerwick, Shetland. An ancient fortress in an unexpectedly suburban setting.
history: Originally built in the Bronze Age as a farmstead, later fortified with an Iron Age broch (tower) around the 1st century BC. The site includes a blockhouse, broch tower, and later wheelhouse structures. Connected to the shore by a causeway. Now surrounded by modern Lerwick housing, creating a striking juxtaposition of ancient and modern. Free to visit and managed by Historic Scotland.

name: Mousa Broch
description: The finest preserved Iron Age broch in the world — a 2,000-year-old stone tower standing 43 feet tall on a tiny Shetland island. An engineering marvel of the ancient world.
history: Built around 100 BC, Mousa Broch is the best-preserved example of these uniquely Scottish structures. Standing 13 metres high with a double-walled construction, it is mentioned in two Norse sagas. The island is also home to a major storm petrel breeding colony, with thousands of birds nesting in the broch walls. Reached by boat from Sandwick. One of Scotland's most extraordinary ancient monuments.

name: Dun Carloway Broch
description: A dramatically situated Iron Age broch on the west coast of Lewis, one of the best-preserved in Scotland. Ancient stonework against a backdrop of Atlantic sky and moorland.
history: Built around 100 BC-100 AD, this Iron Age broch stands on a rocky knoll overlooking Loch Roag on the Isle of Lewis. Survives to a height of over 9 metres on one side, clearly showing the double-wall construction with internal staircase. According to local tradition, it was last used as a refuge by the Morrisons from a raid by the MacAulays. A visitor centre provides interpretation.

name: Amhuinnsuidhe Castle
description: A baronial fantasy on the shores of West Loch Tarbert in Harris — a Victorian shooting lodge in one of Britain's most spectacular and remote settings.
history: Built in 1865-68 by the Earl of Dunmore in Scottish baronial style as a hunting and fishing lodge. Designed by architect David Bryce. J.M. Barrie visited and is said to have been inspired to write 'Mary Rose' here. The castle sits at the foot of mountains beside a salmon river, surrounded by some of the most dramatic scenery in the Outer Hebrides. Now operates as an exclusive-use lodge.

name: Lochore Castle
description: Ruins of a medieval castle on an island in Loch Ore, now part of Lochore Meadows Country Park in Fife. A castle reclaimed from industrial desolation.
history: A 12th-century castle built on an island in Loch Ore by the de Lochore family. The loch was partly drained by mining operations in the 19th and 20th centuries. After the mines closed, the area was transformed into Lochore Meadows Country Park — one of Scotland's great reclamation success stories. The castle ruins are accessible within the park.

name: Loch Doon Castle
description: A remarkable 13th-century curtain wall castle, moved stone by stone from its original island site when the loch was dammed. Eleven-sided walls in wild Ayrshire moorland.
history: Originally built on an island in Loch Doon in the late 13th century, with an unusual eleven-sided curtain wall. When the loch level was raised for hydroelectric power in the 1930s, the castle was dismantled and rebuilt on the shore. Associated with the Wars of Independence — Robert the Bruce may have taken refuge here. Set in remote Galloway Forest countryside.

name: Rowallan Castle
description: A striking medieval tower house near Kilmarnock, ancestral home of the Mure family. A rare example of a 13th-century hall house with later tower additions.
history: Dating from the 13th century, one of Scotland's oldest surviving domestic buildings. Home of the Mure family — Elizabeth Mure married Robert the Steward (later Robert II), making Rowallan an ancestral home of the Stewart dynasty. The castle combines a rare 13th-century hall with a 16th-century tower. Recently restored. Located near Kilmarnock in Ayrshire.

name: Dunfermline Palace
description: Royal palace and abbey complex in Dunfermline — birthplace of Charles I and burial place of Robert the Bruce. Scotland's ancient capital, where kings were born and laid to rest.
history: Founded as a Benedictine priory by Queen Margaret around 1070, later elevated to abbey status. The adjacent royal palace was the birthplace of Charles I in 1600. Robert the Bruce's remains were discovered here in 1818 — his skeleton confirmed the historical account of his embalmed heart being taken on crusade. The abbey nave survives as one of the finest Romanesque churches in Scotland.

name: Finlaystone Castle
description: A country house estate on the Clyde near Langbank, home to the Cunninghame-Graham family. Woodlands, gardens, and connections to both John Knox and Robert Burns.
history: The estate has medieval origins and was held by various families including the Dennistouns and Cunninghames. John Knox is said to have celebrated one of the first Protestant communions here. The current house dates mainly from the 18th-19th centuries. Robert Cunninghame Graham, the writer, traveller, and first president of the Scottish Labour Party, lived here. Now an events and garden venue.

name: Alloway Auld Kirk
description: The ruined church immortalised by Robert Burns in 'Tam o' Shanter' — where Tam watched witches dance by candlelight. Scotland's most famous literary ruin.
history: A 16th-century parish church, already in ruins by Burns' time. Burns' father William Burnes is buried in the kirkyard. In the poem 'Tam o' Shanter' (1790), the drunken Tam spies witches and warlocks dancing here. Adjacent to the Burns Birthplace Museum and within walking distance of the Brig o' Doon. One of Scotland's most important literary pilgrimage sites.

name: Turnberry Castle
description: Fragmentary ruins on the Ayrshire coast, birthplace of Robert the Bruce. Little remains of this pivotal site, but the views across to Ailsa Craig and Arran are magnificent.
history: Traditionally the birthplace of Robert the Bruce in 1274. The castle was destroyed by Bruce himself in 1310 to prevent English use. Very little survives — fragments of wall on a rocky promontory beside the famous Turnberry golf course and lighthouse. The site's importance lies in its Bruce connection rather than its physical remains. Views to Ailsa Craig and the Isle of Arran.

name: Barcaldine Castle
description: The 'Black Castle' of Barcaldine — a restored 16th-century Campbell tower house near Loch Creran. Scene of the treacherous murder that led to the Appin Murder.
history: Built in 1609 by Sir Duncan Campbell of Glenorchy ('Black Duncan'). Known as the Black Castle. Sir Colin Campbell of Glenure, the 'Red Fox' whose murder in 1752 inspired Robert Louis Stevenson's 'Kidnapped', lived here. The castle was restored from ruin in the early 20th century. Now operates as a bed and breakfast. Set amid beautiful Argyll scenery near Benderloch.

name: Aboyne Castle
description: A baronial mansion set in extensive grounds near Aboyne on Royal Deeside. The Marquess of Huntly's seat, with the famous Aboyne Highland Games held on its grounds.
history: The estate has been associated with the Gordon family since the 15th century. The present castle dates mainly from the 19th century, incorporating earlier elements. The grounds host the annual Aboyne Highland Games, one of Scotland's premier Highland gatherings. Set on the banks of the River Dee in beautiful Royal Deeside. Private residence.

name: Tillycairn Castle
description: A small restored tower house near Cluny in Aberdeenshire. A compact baronial gem in the northeast Scotland countryside.
history: A 16th-century tower house, restored and privately inhabited. One of the many minor tower houses that characterise the Aberdeenshire landscape. The restoration preserved original features while making the building habitable. Set in farmland in the Garioch district.

name: Knock Castle
description: Ruined castle on the shores of Loch Goil, Argyll. A Campbell stronghold with dark legends of spectral appearances and clan treachery.
history: A 15th-century castle on the Cowal Peninsula, held by various families before passing to the Campbells. The ruins stand near the shores of Loch Goil. Associated with supernatural legends and stories of clan warfare. The tower survives to a reasonable height. Set in the dramatic landscape of the Argyll Forest Park.

name: Balfluig Castle
description: A beautifully restored 16th-century tower house near Alford in Aberdeenshire. Compact and perfectly proportioned, a jewel of northeast Scotland's castle country.
history: Built in 1556 by the Forbes family, a classic rectangular tower house with turrets and corbelling. Fell into disrepair but was restored in the 20th century by the architect W. Douglas Simpson. Features include a vaulted ground floor, original gun loops, and a fine parapet. Set near the village of Alford in the scenic Howe of Alford. Private residence.

name: Castle of Fiddes
description: A restored 16th-century tower house in the Mearns, Aberdeenshire. Rescued from near-collapse and brought back to life as a family home.
history: Built in 1592 by the Thompson family, a substantial L-plan tower house with turrets. Fell into severe ruin before being restored by the architect Andrew Jameson in the 20th century. Features original carved panels and architectural details. Set in the gentle farmland of the Mearns near Stonehaven. Private residence.

name: Muchalls Castle
description: A 17th-century tower house near Stonehaven with magnificent plaster ceilings and an overmantle of the Royal Arms. Baronial splendour above dramatic coastal cliffs.
history: Built between 1619 and 1627 by the Burnett family (also builders of Crathes Castle). Features remarkable decorative plasterwork ceilings — among the finest in northeast Scotland — and a great hall overmantle displaying the Royal Arms. The castle stands near dramatic coastal cliffs south of Stonehaven. Smugglers' tunnels allegedly connect the castle to the coast. Private but occasionally open.

name: Craigston Castle
description: A distinctive 17th-century castle in Buchan with an unusual arched balcony and carved panels. The Urquhart family seat for over 400 years.
history: Built in 1604-07 by John Urquhart, tutor of Cromarty. Notable for its unique arched gallery connecting two projecting wings, and carved panels depicting biblical and classical scenes. The Urquhart family has owned the castle continuously since its construction. Features fine original architectural details. Located near Turriff in Buchan. Open by appointment.

name: Sadell Castle
description: A restored tower house on the Kintyre coast, overlooking Sadell Bay towards Arran. Now a Landmark Trust holiday let in an idyllic Argyll setting.
history: Built around 1508 by David Hamilton, Bishop of Argyll, near the ruins of Saddell Abbey (founded by Somerled in 1160). A rectangular tower house that fell into ruin but was restored by the Landmark Trust as holiday accommodation. Set on the beautiful east coast of Kintyre with views to the Isle of Arran. The adjacent abbey ruins and medieval grave slabs are also worth exploring.

name: Carbisdale Castle
description: A flamboyant Edwardian castle overlooking the Kyle of Sutherland, built as an act of spite by a duchess snubbed by the establishment. Scotland's most extravagant revenge.
history: Built 1906-17 for Mary Caroline, Duchess of Sutherland, after a bitter legal battle over her husband's will. She positioned the castle's clock tower with faces on only three sides — the fourth facing the Sutherland estates was left blank as a final insult. Served as a youth hostel for decades. Features impressive marble staircase and baronial grandeur. Currently closed awaiting new use.

name: Skibo Castle
description: Andrew Carnegie's magnificent Highland retreat, now one of Scotland's most exclusive private members' clubs. Where the steel magnate became a Scottish laird.
history: Medieval origins, but transformed 1898-1903 by Andrew Carnegie, the Scottish-American steel magnate, into a lavish Edwardian country house. Carnegie spent his summers here and entertained guests including Edward VII. Madonna married here in 2000. Now operates as the Carnegie Club, an ultra-exclusive private members' club. Set on 7,500 acres overlooking the Dornoch Firth.

name: Castle Leod
description: The seat of Clan Mackenzie in Strathpeffer, said to have inspired Diana Gabaldon's 'Castle Leoch' in the Outlander novels. A living clan castle with ancient trees.
history: Dating from the 15th century, the castle has been the seat of the Mackenzie Earls of Cromartie for over 500 years. Features include two ancient Spanish chestnut trees reputedly planted from seeds brought back from the Battle of Poitiers. Diana Gabaldon acknowledged Castle Leod as partial inspiration for Castle Leoch in her 'Outlander' series. Open for clan gatherings and limited tours.

name: Dunure Castle
description: Ruined Kennedy stronghold on the Ayrshire coast, where the Earl of Cassillis roasted the Commendator of Crossraguel Abbey alive to steal church lands. Dark deeds above a pretty harbour.
history: A 13th-century castle perched on a rocky headland above the fishing village of Dunure. Most infamously, in 1570, Gilbert Kennedy, 4th Earl of Cassillis, roasted Allan Stewart, Commendator of Crossraguel Abbey, in the 'black vault' here to force him to sign over abbey lands. The ruins overlook a picturesque harbour with views across to Arran and Ailsa Craig.

name: Balloch Castle
description: A Gothic Revival castle set in a country park on the southern shore of Loch Lomond. The gateway to the Highlands, with stunning lochside grounds.
history: Built in 1808 for John Buchanan of Ardoch in Gothic Revival style, replacing an earlier castle. The grounds became Balloch Castle Country Park in 1980 — 200 acres of gardens, woodland, and loch shore. The walled garden and formal grounds are particularly fine. The castle serves as a visitor centre. Located at the southern tip of Loch Lomond, Scotland's largest loch.

name: Cambuskenneth Abbey
description: A hauntingly beautiful ruined abbey near Stirling, with a soaring detached bell tower. The burial place of James III and his queen — Scotland's lost royal mausoleum.
history: Founded in 1147 by David I as an Augustinian priory. Robert the Bruce held parliament here in 1326. James III and his queen Margaret of Denmark were buried here in 1488. The detached bell tower (13th century) is one of the finest in Scotland. Most of the abbey was demolished after the Reformation. The tomb of James III was rediscovered in 1864 and marked by Queen Victoria.

name: Balgonie Castle
description: A remarkably complete medieval castle near Markinch in Fife, still inhabited and lovingly maintained by the Laird of Balgonie. Living history with a personal touch.
history: The tower dates from the late 14th century, built by Sir Thomas Sibbald. Extended over the centuries with a great hall, chapel, and courtyard. Rob Roy MacGregor occupied the castle briefly in 1716. Now owned by the Morris family who have been restoring and living in it since 1985. Open for visits, weddings, and medieval events. One of Scotland's most atmospheric occupied castles.

name: St Bridget's Kirk, Dalgety
description: A ruined medieval church on the Fife coast, with a rare post-Reformation laird's loft. Peaceful ruins above the Firth of Forth.
history: A 12th-century parish church dedicated to St Bridget, built on a site with possible early Christian origins. Features include a rare surviving laird's loft (a private gallery added after the Reformation by the Earl of Dunfermline) and medieval grave slabs. Set in a peaceful churchyard overlooking the Firth of Forth near Aberdour. Managed by Historic Environment Scotland.

name: Red Castle
description: Ruined tower house on the shores of Lunan Bay in Angus — one of Scotland's most beautiful beaches. Red sandstone fragments against white sand and blue sea.
history: Built in the 12th century as a royal castle by William the Lion. The red sandstone tower (which gives it its name) dates from the 15th century when it was held by the Stewart and then the Gray families. The castle overlooks the magnificent sweep of Lunan Bay, one of Scotland's finest beaches. The ruins are atmospheric and freely accessible, perfect for combining with a beach visit.

name: Restenneth Priory
description: An ancient priory near Forfar with a Romanesque tower that may be one of the oldest standing structures in Scotland. Deep roots in Pictish and early Christian history.
history: Founded in the 8th century, possibly on the site of a church established by St Boniface. The lower stages of the tower are Romanesque, possibly dating to the 11th century, making it one of Scotland's oldest standing buildings. The priory was refounded as an Augustinian house in the 12th century. Robert, infant son of Robert the Bruce, was buried here. Atmospheric ruins in flat Angus farmland.

name: Kinloss Abbey
description: Fragmentary remains of a Cistercian abbey near Forres in Moray. Founded by David I, now mostly vanished but with an air of ancient sanctity.
history: Founded in 1150 by King David I for Cistercian monks. The abbey became wealthy and influential, with extensive lands in Moray. The Italian scholar Giovanni Ferrerio taught here in the 16th century, making it a centre of Renaissance learning. Largely demolished after the Reformation, with stones used for local building. Only fragments survive, set in a quiet location near Kinloss village.

name: Birnie Kirk
description: One of Scotland's oldest churches still in regular use — a beautiful Romanesque kirk near Elgin that has served its parish for nearly 900 years.
history: Built around 1140, Birnie Kirk served as the cathedral of the Diocese of Moray before the see was moved to Elgin.
