The simple Romanesque church has thick walls, a round chancel arch, and a peaceful atmosphere. A rare Celtic bell (the Birnie Bell) was found here. One of the best-preserved Romanesque churches in Scotland, still holding regular Church of Scotland services.

name: Darnaway Castle
description: Seat of the Earls of Moray, famous for its magnificent 15th-century hammerbeam roof — one of only two medieval hammerbeam roofs surviving in Scotland.
history: The estate has been held by the Earls of Moray since the 14th century. The original castle included a great hall with a remarkable hammerbeam oak roof, built around 1450 and surviving within the current 19th-century building. Mary Queen of Scots visited in 1564. The castle was rebuilt in the Gothic Revival style in 1802 but the medieval hall was preserved. Private, rarely open.

name: Troup Head Castle
description: Fragmentary remains of a coastal fortification near the RSPB gannet colony at Troup Head, Aberdeenshire. The birds now outnumber the stones.
history: Minimal remains of a castle or fortification on the dramatic cliffs at Troup Head. The headland is now famous for its mainland gannet colony — one of very few in Scotland — managed by the RSPB. The castle remains are secondary to the spectacular coastal scenery and wildlife. Located near Pennan on the Banffshire coast.

name: Kincardine Castle
description: Fragmentary ruins of a once-important royal castle in the Howe of the Mearns, near Fettercairn. Scotland's forgotten royal residence.
history: A major royal castle from the 12th century, used as a residence by several Scottish kings. Kenneth II is said to have been murdered here in 995. The castle gave its name to the historic county of Kincardineshire. Very little survives above ground — the site was quarried for building material. Located near Fettercairn in the agricultural Mearns.

name: Dunecht House Chapel
description: A magnificent private chapel attached to Dunecht House in Aberdeenshire, noted for its remarkable art and architecture. Gothic Revival splendour in a secret setting.
history: Built in the 1870s for the 25th Earl of Crawford, who filled it with priceless Italian Renaissance art. The chapel made headlines in 1881 when the earl's embalmed body was stolen from its vault — the bizarre crime was not solved for months. The chapel features exceptional stained glass, carved woodwork, and marble. Part of the private Dunecht estate near Westhill.

name: Castle Roy
description: One of Scotland's oldest surviving castles — a simple 12th-century curtain wall enclosure near Nethy Bridge in Strathspey. Bare and ancient, with a Comyn ghost.
history: A remarkably early stone castle, dating from the late 12th century. One of the oldest surviving stone castles in the Highlands. A simple rectangular enclosure with a projecting tower, built by the Comyn lords of Badenoch. Minimal decoration or domestic refinement — this is castle architecture at its most primal. Set near Nethy Bridge with views to the Cairngorms. Freely accessible.

name: Seton Collegiate Church
description: Scotland's finest medieval collegiate church, beautifully preserved near the East Lothian coast. Exquisite Gothic architecture with an unfinished nave that speaks of ambitions cut short.
history: Founded in 1492 by the Seton family, one of Scotland's most powerful medieval dynasties. The church features superb Gothic vaulting, carved sedilia, and the apse with its rib-vaulted ceiling is among the finest medieval architecture in Scotland. Mary Queen of Scots attended a celebration here after Darnley's murder. The nave was never completed. Managed by Historic Environment Scotland.

name: Amisfield Tower
description: A fine 16th-century tower house near Dumfries, one of the best-preserved in southwest Scotland. Charrier family ambition rendered in warm sandstone.
history: Built around 1600 by the Charteris (Charrier) family. A tall rectangular tower house with corbelled turrets and a parapet walk. One of the most complete tower houses in Dumfriesshire. The castle later passed to various families. Set in the gentle Nithsdale landscape. Well-preserved exterior with limited access to the interior.

name: Whithorn Priory
description: Scotland's cradle of Christianity — where St Ninian built his 'Candida Casa' in the 5th century. The earliest known Christian site in Scotland, still resonant with ancient sanctity.
history: Tradition holds that St Ninian founded a church here around 397 AD, making Whithorn the earliest Christian settlement in Scotland. The priory was established in the 12th century by Premonstratensian canons. An important medieval pilgrimage destination — even Scottish kings made the journey. Excavations have revealed continuous occupation from the 5th century. The Whithorn Trust museum displays remarkable finds.

name: Cruggleton Castle
description: Fragmentary remains of a Norman castle on the Galloway coast, with commanding views over Wigtown Bay. Once a stronghold of the Comyns.
history: A 12th-century castle built by the Comyn family on a headland above Wigtown Bay. The nearby Cruggleton Church has a fine Norman doorway. The castle site has mostly eroded into the sea, with only fragments remaining. The coastal setting is beautiful, with views across the bay. Part of the network of Norman-era fortifications in Galloway.

name: Corsewall Lighthouse
description: A working lighthouse on Scotland's southwestern tip, now a luxury hotel. Robert Stevenson engineering meets boutique hospitality, with views to Ireland and the Isle of Man.
history: Built in 1815 by Robert Stevenson (grandfather of Robert Louis Stevenson) for the Northern Lighthouse Board. One of the first lighthouses built on the mainland Scottish coast. The tower and keepers' cottages have been converted into a hotel and restaurant. The light is still operational. Located on the Rhins of Galloway with views across the Irish Sea to Belfast and the Isle of Man.

name: Drumcoltran Tower
description: A well-preserved 16th-century tower house in rural Dumfriesshire, with thick walls and a vaulted ground floor. Quiet dignity in the farming heartland.
history: Built around 1570 by the Maxwell family. A substantial rectangular tower house with walls up to 8 feet thick. The tower retains its barrel-vaulted ground floor and much of its original fabric. Passed through various families. Now in the care of Historic Environment Scotland. Set in peaceful Dumfriesshire farmland near the village of Kirkgunzeon.

name: Old Scatness
description: A remarkable multi-period archaeological site near Sumburgh in Shetland, spanning 3,000 years from the Bronze Age to the Vikings. Sister site to nearby Jarlshof.
history: Excavated from the 1990s, revealing an Iron Age broch, Pictish wheelhouses, and Viking longhouses. Together with nearby Jarlshof, it demonstrates the extraordinary depth of settlement at Shetland's southern tip. Features interactive reconstructions including a roundhouse. The site complements Jarlshof brilliantly and helps explain the complex layers of Shetland's past.

name: Windhouse
description: Reputedly the most haunted house in Shetland, a ruined Georgian mansion on the island of Yell. Bleak, windswept, and deliciously sinister.
history: Built in 1707, Windhouse was occupied until the 1920s and has stood empty since. Numerous ghost stories surround the building — a skeleton was found behind a wall during renovation work, and a woman in silk, a tall man, and a spectral dog are all said to haunt the premises. The ruin stands exposed on the moorland of Yell, one of Shetland's northern islands.

name: Borve Castle
description: A ruined medieval fortress on the north coast of Sutherland, overlooking the wild Borve beach. A lonely sentinel in one of Scotland's most remote landscapes.
history: A medieval castle, probably dating from the 14th century, associated with the Mackay clan. The ruins stand on the coast near the village of Farr in Sutherland, overlooking beautiful Borve beach. The castle controlled the surrounding territory of Strathnaver. Very little is known of its specific history. The setting is strikingly remote and beautiful.

name: Varrich Castle
description: A lonely tower ruin high above the Kyle of Tongue in Sutherland, with one of the finest viewpoints in the Scottish Highlands. A short steep climb for an unforgettable panorama.
history: Probably dating from the 14th century, associated with the Mackay clan who controlled Strathnaver. Perched on a rocky knoll high above the Kyle of Tongue with breathtaking views to Ben Hope, Ben Loyal, and the northern Highlands. The small tower is fragmentary but the site is unforgettable. A popular short walk from the village of Tongue.

name: Ardtornish Castle
description: Ruined stronghold of the MacDonald Lords of the Isles on the Sound of Mull. Where the Treaty of Ardtornish was signed — an audacious pact to divide Scotland with England.
history: A 13th-century castle on a promontory overlooking the Sound of Mull. In 1462, the Treaty of Westminster-Ardtornish was secretly signed here between John MacDonald, Lord of the Isles, and Edward IV of England, agreeing to divide Scotland between them. When discovered, the treaty led to the forfeiture of the Lordship. The atmospheric ruins are reached by a walk along the shore from Lochaline.

name: Tor Castle
description: Minimal ruins of a Cameron stronghold near Fort William, hidden in woodland above the River Lochy. The ancient seat of the chiefs before they moved to Achnacarry.
history: The original seat of the Cameron chiefs, dating from the medieval period. The Camerons moved to Achnacarry Castle in the 17th century. Very little remains of the structure, hidden in woodland near Torlundy above the River Lochy. The site is of more historical than visual interest, marking the origins of one of the great Highland clans.

name: Achanduin Castle
description: A 13th-century curtain wall castle on the Isle of Lismore, commanding views over Loch Linnhe. Once seat of the Bishops of Argyll in a stunningly beautiful island setting.
history: Built in the 13th century as the residence of the Bishops of Argyll. A rectangular curtain wall castle on a rocky knoll on the Isle of Lismore, with views across Loch Linnhe to the Morvern Hills. The island of Lismore was an important early Christian centre. The castle ruins are well-preserved and freely accessible. Reached by ferry from Oban or Port Appin.

name: Ardchattan Priory
description: Romantic ruins of a Valliscaulian priory on the shore of Loch Etive, with fine medieval grave slabs. One of only three houses of this rare French monastic order in Scotland.
history: Founded in 1230 by Duncan MacDougall for Valliscaulian monks — one of only three such houses in Scotland (with Beauly and Pluscarden). Robert the Bruce held a parliament here in 1308. The priory was burned by Cromwell's troops in 1654. The ruins include fine carved grave slabs in the chapter house. Adjacent Ardchattan House has a beautiful garden open to visitors.

name: Kilmartin Church
description: A parish church at the heart of Kilmartin Glen — Scotland's richest prehistoric landscape — housing an outstanding collection of medieval carved grave slabs.
history: The current church dates from 1835, but it houses a remarkable collection of medieval West Highland carved grave slabs and crosses. Kilmartin Glen itself contains over 350 prehistoric and historic monuments within a six-mile radius, including cairns, standing stones, and rock art spanning 5,000 years. The adjacent Kilmartin Museum (reopened 2023) interprets this extraordinary concentration of ancient sites.

name: Kilwinning Abbey
description: Romantic ruins of a medieval abbey in the heart of Kilwinning, Ayrshire. Claimed as the birthplace of Scottish Freemasonry and home to a famous annual papingo shoot.
history: Founded around 1162 by Tironensian monks, possibly by Hugh de Morville. The abbey was a major religious house in medieval Ayrshire. Legend connects it with the origins of Scottish Freemasonry — Lodge Mother Kilwinning No. 0 claims to be the oldest Masonic lodge in the world. An annual papingo (parrot) shooting contest has been held from the abbey tower since medieval times. Substantial ruins survive.

name: Kilmun Church
description: A historic church on Holy Loch in the Cowal Peninsula, with the mausoleum of the Dukes of Argyll. An atmospheric spot where Campbell chiefs rest among ancient yews.
history: The site has ancient Christian origins — St Munn (Fintan Munnu) established a church here in the 7th century. The current church dates from 1841 but incorporates a medieval tower. The Argyll Mausoleum holds the remains of the Campbell chiefs and Dukes of Argyll. The churchyard contains fine carved stones and a Celtic cross. Beautifully situated on the shores of Holy Loch.

name: Innerpeffray Chapel
description: A medieval collegiate chapel near Crieff, adjacent to Scotland's oldest free public lending library. A double treasure of Scottish heritage in rural Perthshire.
history: Founded in 1508 by Lord Drummond as a collegiate chapel. The adjacent Innerpeffray Library, founded in 1680, is Scotland's oldest free public lending library, with a collection including rare Bibles and Gaelic manuscripts. The chapel retains medieval features and is set in a peaceful location above the River Earn. Both chapel and library are open to visitors.

name: Buittle Castle
description: Earthwork remains of a significant medieval castle near Dalbeattie, Galloway. The Balliol family seat — birthplace of a king, now just green mounds in a quiet field.
history: A major medieval castle of the Balliol family — John Balliol, who became King of Scotland in 1292, was associated with this site. The castle was an important centre of lordship in Galloway. Now only earthworks survive — a motte and bailey in farmland near the village of Buittle. The site's historical significance far outweighs its physical remains.

name: Tibbers Castle
description: Remains of a medieval castle in Upper Nithsdale, with earthworks and fragmentary walls above the River Nith.
history: A 13th-century castle held by the Comyns, later captured by the English during the Wars of Independence. William Wallace's forces attacked it in 1298. The castle was garrisoned by the English until retaken by Robert the Bruce. Only earthworks and wall fragments survive, set above the River Nith near Thornhill in Dumfriesshire.

name: Closeburn Castle
description: One of Scotland's oldest continuously inhabited castles — a massive 14th-century tower in Nithsdale, held by the Kirkpatricks for 500 years.
history: The tower dates from around 1350 and has been continuously inhabited since. The Kirkpatrick family, whose ancestor Roger de Kirkpatrick reputedly finished off the Red Comyn after Robert the Bruce's attack, held the castle for five centuries. One of the earliest stone tower houses in Scotland. Massive walls and a well-preserved interior. Private but visible from the road near Thornhill.

name: Nithsdale Castle
description: A castle site in the Nith valley of Dumfriesshire. Limited remains in the agricultural landscape of upper Nithsdale.
history: A medieval castle site in the Nith valley, associated with the various lordships that controlled this important routeway between England and central Scotland. Limited remains survive. The Nith valley was a major invasion route and saw frequent conflict during the Wars of Independence.

name: Fortingall Church
description: A tiny Highland church in Glen Lyon sheltering the Fortingall Yew — possibly the oldest living thing in Europe, estimated at 3,000-9,000 years old.
history: The church dates from the 19th century but stands on an ancient Christian site. The Fortingall Yew in the churchyard is estimated to be between 3,000 and 9,000 years old, making it potentially the oldest tree in Europe. Local legend claims Pontius Pilate was born in Fortingall. The village has charming thatched cottages designed by James MacLaren in the 1890s. Set in beautiful Glen Lyon.

name: Auchinleck House
description: The Georgian mansion of James Boswell, biographer of Dr Johnson. Literary pilgrimage meets Ayrshire country house elegance, rescued from near-ruin by a landmark restoration.
history: Built in the 1760s for Lord Auchinleck, father of James Boswell. Dr Samuel Johnson visited in 1773 during his Highland tour with Boswell. The house fell into serious disrepair by the late 20th century. Rescued by the Scottish Historic Buildings Trust and the Landmark Trust, who restored it as holiday accommodation. A Palladian villa in the Ayrshire countryside.

name: Portencross Castle
description: A restored 14th-century castle on the Ayrshire coast, overlooking the Isle of Arran and Little Cumbrae. Where Scottish kings embarked for their island coronations.
history: Built in the 14th century by the Boyd family. Tradition holds that Scottish kings departed from here to be crowned on the sacred island of Iona. Served as a keeper's castle for the Royal Forest of Arnele. Damaged in the great storm of 1739 that also wrecked Alloway Kirk. Recently restored by the Friends of Portencross Castle and open to visitors.

name: Kinross House
description: Scotland's finest example of Palladian architecture, designed by Sir William Bruce. Elegant classical grandeur beside Loch Leven, with views to the island castle where Mary Queen of Scots was imprisoned.
history: Built 1686-93 by Sir William Bruce, architect of the Palace of Holyroodhouse, as his own residence. Considered the first significant Palladian house in Scotland. Designed to frame views of Loch Leven Castle, where Mary Queen of Scots was imprisoned. The formal gardens were restored in the 21st century. Private but gardens and grounds open seasonally.

name: Leuchars Norman Church
description: The finest Romanesque church in Scotland — St Athernase's apse and chancel are a masterwork of 12th-century carved stonework. A medieval jewel in a Fife village.
history: The church of St Athernase dates from the late 12th century and features an extraordinary apse with two tiers of interlaced arcading — the finest Romanesque architectural detail surviving in Scotland. The corbel table includes carved heads and animals. The nave was rebuilt in the 17th century. Located in the village of Leuchars near RAF Leuchars and St Andrews.

name: Dysart Church
description: Historic church and harbour area in the picturesque Fife town of Dysart, with connections to early Christianity and the town's trading past.
history: The church and surrounding historic area of Dysart have medieval origins. St Serf is said to have lived in a cave here in the 6th century. The town was an important medieval trading port, exporting coal and salt to the Low Countries. The Pan Ha' group of restored 17th-century merchant houses is one of the finest streetscapes in Scotland. The harbour area is now beautifully restored by the National Trust for Scotland.

name: Dunimarle Castle
description: A Georgian mansion in Culross, Fife, set in parkland above the Firth of Forth. Private estate with connections to the Erskine family.
history: The present house dates from the 18th century, built on a site with older origins. Associated with the Erskine family. Set in attractive grounds near the historic Royal Burgh of Culross. The nearby village of Culross, with its palace and medieval streets, is one of Scotland's best-preserved historic towns. Private residence.

name: Kinneff Church
description: A small Mearns church where the Scottish Crown Jewels were hidden during Cromwell's occupation — one of the most daring acts of defiance in Scottish history.
history: In 1652, the Honours of Scotland (Crown, Sceptre, and Sword of State) were smuggled out of Dunnottar Castle during Cromwell's siege and hidden under the floor of Kinneff Church by the minister's wife. They remained buried here for eight years until the Restoration in 1660. The current church dates from 1738 but stands on the medieval site. A monument in the churchyard commemorates the rescue.

name: Deer Abbey
description: Ruins of a Cistercian abbey in Buchan, associated with the famous Book of Deer — one of Scotland's oldest surviving manuscripts, containing the earliest known Gaelic text from Scotland.
history: Founded in 1219 by William Comyn, Earl of Buchan, for Cistercian monks. The abbey is associated with the Book of Deer, a 10th-century gospel book with marginal notes in Scots Gaelic — the earliest surviving Gaelic text from Scotland. The ruins include walls of the church, cloister, and domestic buildings. Set in peaceful Buchan countryside near Mintlaw.

name: Monymusk Priory Church
description: A beautifully preserved Romanesque church in the Aberdeenshire village of Monymusk, associated with the legendary Brecbennoch — the relic carried before Bruce's army at Bannockburn.
history: Founded as an Augustinian priory in the 12th century. The church retains fine Romanesque architectural features. Associated with the Brecbennoch of St Columba — a reliquary that was carried before Robert the Bruce's army at Bannockburn in 1314 (now identified as the Monymusk Reliquary in the National Museum of Scotland). Still in use as the parish church of Monymusk.

name: Kinkell Church
description: A ruined medieval church near Inverurie in Aberdeenshire, notable for a remarkably fine sacrament house — one of the best examples of pre-Reformation church furnishing in Scotland.
history: A medieval parish church with a sacrament house dated 1524, featuring elaborate carved Gothic tracery and an inscription. One of the finest surviving examples of this type of medieval church furnishing in Scotland. The church was abandoned in the 18th century when the parish was united with nearby Keith Hall. The ruins are atmospheric and freely accessible.

name: Tillyangus Castle
description: A restored tower house in the Aberdeenshire countryside, near the village of Clatt. Compact baronial charm in the Garioch.
history: A 16th-century tower house, restored and privately inhabited. One of the many minor tower houses that characterise the Aberdeenshire landscape. Features typical northeast Scottish architectural details including turrets and crow-stepped gables. Set in farmland in the Garioch district near the village of Clatt.

name: Leith Hall
description: A charming family home spanning 350 years, with a beautiful walled garden and exhibition of the Leith-Hay family's military adventures. National Trust for Scotland property.
history: Built from 1650 onwards by the Leith family (later Leith-Hay), with each generation adding to the house. The family had a strong military tradition — seven successive lairds served in the army. The house contains fascinating personal memorabilia, and the grounds include a walled garden, woodland walks, and a bird observation hide. Managed by the National Trust for Scotland.

name: Haddo House
description: An elegant Georgian mansion in Aberdeenshire designed by William Adam, home to the Gordons of Haddo. Palladian grace, Pre-Raphaelite chapel, and magnificent parkland.
history: Designed by William Adam in 1732 for William Gordon, 2nd Earl of Aberdeen. The interiors were remodelled in the 1880s with rich Adam Revival decoration. Features include a chapel with stained glass by Burne-Jones. The 4th Earl of Aberdeen served as Prime Minister 1852-55. The grounds include a country park with walks and wildlife. Managed by the National Trust for Scotland.

name: Pitmedden Garden
description: A spectacular recreation of a 17th-century formal garden in Aberdeenshire, with 40,000 bedding plants in intricate parterre designs. Baroque symmetry in the Scottish northeast.
history: The Great Garden was created in 1675 by Sir Alexander Seton, 1st Baronet of Pitmedden. After falling into disrepair, the National Trust for Scotland recreated the elaborate parterre gardens in the 1950s using historical sources. Features four parterres with designs based on those at Holyroodhouse. Also includes a museum of farming life. A unique and colourful attraction.

name: Dalmeny Church
description: The finest and most complete Romanesque church in Scotland, near Edinburgh. An astonishing 12th-century survivor with a richly carved south doorway and apse.
history: Built around 1160, Dalmeny Church is the best-preserved Romanesque (Norman) church in Scotland. The south doorway features extraordinary carved decoration including animals, foliage, and interlaced patterns. The apse with its corbel table of carved heads is also remarkable. Still in use as a parish church. Located in the village of Dalmeny, near the Forth Bridge.

name: Cramond Tower
description: A 15th-century tower house in the picturesque village of Cramond, Edinburgh. Medieval fortification in a charming riverside setting where the Almond meets the Forth.
history: Built in the 15th century, possibly as a residence associated with the Bishops of Dunkeld. The tower stands in the historic village of Cramond, where the River Almond meets the Firth of Forth. Cramond also has significant Roman remains — a fort and bathhouse. The village is one of Edinburgh's most attractive suburbs, popular for walks along the shore.

name: Merchiston Castle
description: The 15th-century tower house where John Napier invented logarithms — now incorporated into Edinburgh Napier University. A castle that changed mathematics forever.
history: Built around 1454 by the Napier family. Most famous as the birthplace and home of John Napier (1550-1617), who invented logarithms and popularised the decimal point — fundamental contributions to mathematics. The L-plan tower house was incorporated into Edinburgh Napier University's Merchiston campus. The tower itself has been restored and can be visited by arrangement.

name: Liberton Tower
description: A restored 15th-century tower house in suburban Edinburgh, incongruously standing amid modern housing. A medieval time capsule in the city outskirts.
history: Built around 1453 by the Dalmahoy family. A simple rectangular tower house, three storeys high with a barrel-vaulted ground floor. Fell into ruin but was restored in the early 2000s. Now stands somewhat incongruously amid modern Edinburgh housing in the suburb of Liberton. One of several surviving medieval towers within Edinburgh's city limits.

name: Castle Craig
description: A ruined castle on the shores of Loch Ness, one of several fortifications along the Great Glen. Hidden in woodland with atmospheric views across the famous loch.
history: A medieval castle site on the shores of Loch Ness. One of several defensive positions along the Great Glen, which served as a major routeway through the Highlands. The ruins are fragmentary and hidden in woodland on the steep banks above the loch. The setting is atmospheric, though the castle is overshadowed by better-known sites like Urquhart Castle further along the loch.

name: Newark Castle (Borders)
description: A ruined tower house in the Yarrow Valley, scene of a terrible massacre of prisoners after the Battle of Philiphaugh. Walter Scott's inspiration for 'The Lay of the Last Minstrel'.
history: A 15th-century tower house in Selkirkshire, held by the Scotts of Buccleuch. In 1645, after the Covenanter victory at Philiphaugh, up to 100 Royalist prisoners — many of them Irish soldiers with their camp followers — were massacred in the castle barmkin. Sir Walter Scott used the castle as inspiration for parts of his poetry. Set in the beautiful Yarrow Valley near Selkirk.

name: Traquair House
description: Scotland's oldest continuously inhabited house — a romantic white-harled mansion in the Tweed valley that has sheltered 27 Scottish monarchs and hidden Catholic priests.
history: Claimed to have been inhabited since 1107, making it Scotland's oldest continuously occupied house. Originally a royal hunting lodge, later the seat of the Stuart Earls of Traquair. Mary Queen of Scots stayed here in 1566. The famous Bear Gates were closed in 1745, with a vow not to reopen until a Stuart sat on the throne again. Features a working brewery, hedge maze, and Catholic chapel. Open to visitors.

name: Balfour Castle
description: A Victorian baronial pile on the Orkney island of Shapinsay, surrounded by formal gardens. Built by the laird who transformed the island's agriculture.
history: Built in 1847 by David Balfour to designs by architect David Bryce. Balfour was a progressive laird who modernised farming on Shapinsay, building roads, a gasworks, and a water supply. The castle is a fine example of Scottish baronial architecture with turrets, crow-stepped gables, and formal gardens. Originally a hotel, now a private residence. Located on the small island of Shapinsay, reached by ferry from Kirkwall.

name: Wyre Heritage Centre
description: A small heritage centre on the Orkney island of Wyre, near the oldest Norse castle in Scotland — Cubbie Roo's Castle — and an atmospheric medieval chapel.
history: The tiny island of Wyre (pop. ~30) holds remarkable heritage: Cubbie Roo's Castle, the oldest dateable stone castle in Scotland (c. 1145), and St Mary's Chapel (12th century). The heritage centre provides interpretation. The castle was built by Kolbein Hruga ('Cubbie Roo'), a Norse chieftain mentioned in the Orkneyinga Saga. Reached by ferry from Rousay.

name: Quoyberstane Stone
description: A Pictish/Norse carved stone in Orkney, one of many carved stones scattered across the islands. A fragment of the deep cultural layers that define Orcadian heritage.
history: A carved stone in Orkney, part of the rich collection of Pictish, Norse, and early Christian carved stones found across the islands. Orkney's strategic position between Scotland and Scandinavia created a unique cultural blend visible in its carved stones, which often combine Celtic, Pictish, and Norse artistic traditions. Of archaeological and art-historical interest.

name: Brahan Castle Site
description: The vanished seat of the Mackenzie Earls of Seaforth, famous for the Brahan Seer — Scotland's most celebrated prophet, who was burned here in a barrel of tar.
history: Brahan Castle was the principal seat of the Mackenzie Earls of Seaforth near Dingwall. The castle was demolished in 1953. Most famous for the legend of the Brahan Seer (Coinneach Odhar), who was executed here (according to tradition, burned in a barrel of tar) for telling the Countess of Seaforth that her husband was being unfaithful. His prophecies continue to fascinate. Only the estate grounds survive.

name: Kilravock Castle
description: The ancestral home of the Rose clan for over 600 years, set in the Highland countryside near Culloden. Bonnie Prince Charlie dined here the night before the battle.
history: The oldest part dates from 1460, built by Hugh Rose, 7th Baron of Kilravock. Bonnie Prince Charlie visited and dined here on 14 April 1746 — the night before Culloden. The Duke of Cumberland called the following day. The Rose family has held the castle continuously since 1293. Features include a 15th-century keep and later baronial additions. Open for B&B and tours.

name: Ardencaple Castle
description: Site of the former MacAulay clan seat near Helensburgh. Demolished in the 19th century, now within the grounds of a naval base. History erased by progress.
history: The ancestral seat of the MacAulay chiefs near Rhu on the Gareloch. The castle dated from the medieval period but was demolished and replaced by various structures. The site now lies within HMNB Clyde/Faslane naval base area. Little or nothing survives of the original castle. Of historical rather than visual interest.

name: Ardchonnel Castle
description: Another island fortress on Loch Awe — a Campbell stronghold on Inishail or a nearby island, part of the web of castles that controlled this vast Highland loch.
history: A medieval castle on an island in Loch Awe, associated with the Campbell lords. One of several island castles on this largest of the Argyll lochs. Fragmentary ruins accessible only by boat. The loch's island castles collectively tell the story of Campbell expansion through the western Highlands. Remote and rarely visited.

name: Castle Loch Heylipol
description: Traces of a crannog castle on the Isle of Tiree, one of the most wind-swept and sun-blessed islands in Scotland.
history: A crannog or island dwelling in a loch on the Isle of Tiree. Minimal remains survive. Tiree is famous for its Atlantic winds, machair wildflowers, and being one of the sunniest places in Scotland. The castle site is of archaeological rather than visual interest, part of the island's long history of habitation.

name: Crarae Garden Castle
description: A woodland garden of extraordinary beauty on Loch Fyne, often called 'the Himalayan Gorge of Scotland'. Rare plants in a dramatic glen setting.
history: Created from 1912 by Grace, Lady Campbell, and extended by her son Sir George Campbell, who collected seeds from plant-hunting expeditions to the Himalayas, Nepal, and China. The garden fills a dramatic glen with waterfalls, featuring rhododendrons, azaleas, and rare tree species. Now managed by the National Trust for Scotland. One of Scotland's finest woodland gardens.

name: Dunderave Castle
description: A restored tower house on the shores of Loch Fyne, beautifully sited with views across the loch. The MacNaughton clan seat, rescued from near-total ruin.
history: Built in 1596 by the MacNaughton clan chief. A classic L-plan tower house on the shores of Loch Fyne near Inveraray. Fell into severe ruin — Neil Munro described it evocatively in his novel 'Doom Castle'. Restored in the early 20th century by Sir Robert Lorimer. Now a private residence in a beautiful waterside setting.

name: Ellangowan Castle
description: A ruined 18th-century mansion near Creetown in Galloway, possibly named after Walter Scott's novel 'Guy Mannering'. Romantic decay in a gentle landscape.
history: An 18th-century mansion house, now ruined. The name may be connected to Sir Walter Scott's novel 'Guy Mannering' (1815), in which the fictional Ellangowan Castle plays a central role. Set in the rolling Galloway countryside near the Cree estuary. The ruins are atmospheric but limited in extent.

name: Carsphairn Castle
description: Minimal remains of a castle in the remote Galloway Hills village of Carsphairn. More significant as a marker of medieval lordship than as a visible ruin.
history: A medieval castle site in the village of Carsphairn in the Galloway Hills. Very little survives above ground. The village sits at the head of the Water of Deugh in a beautiful upland landscape. The Galloway Hills offer some of Scotland's finest and least-visited hill walking.

name: Lag Tower
description: Ruins of a Grierson stronghold in Nithsdale, associated with 'Lag' — the notorious persecutor of Covenanters. Dark history in the Dumfriesshire hills.
history: Home of Sir Robert Grierson of Lag (1655-1733), one of the most feared persecutors of Covenanters during the 'Killing Time' of the 1680s. Grierson was so hated that legend claimed the Devil attended his funeral. The tower ruins stand in the Nithsdale countryside near Dunscore. A site loaded with dark Covenanting history.

name: Abergeldie Castle
description: A 16th-century tower house on Royal Deeside, near Balmoral. Long associated with the Royal Family as a guest house, perched above the eroding banks of the River Dee.
history: Built around 1550 by the Gordon family. Leased to the British Royal Family from the mid-19th century as overflow accommodation for Balmoral guests. Queen Victoria's mother lived here. In recent years, the castle has been threatened by severe erosion of the River Dee bank, prompting major engineering work to save it. A Gordon stronghold with deep royal connections.

name: Crovie Village
description: A single row of houses clinging to the foot of sea cliffs in Banffshire — one of Scotland's most extraordinary villages. No road access, just a footpath between cliff and sea.
history: A tiny fishing village on the Moray Firth coast, accessible only on foot. Houses are squeezed between cliff and shore, so narrow that there's no room for cars. Largely abandoned after a devastating storm in 1953, some cottages have since been restored as holiday lets. Sister village to the equally dramatic Pennan (featured in the film 'Local Hero'). One of Scotland's most unforgettable places.

name: Pennan Village
description: The tiny Banffshire fishing village made famous by the film 'Local Hero'. A single row of whitewashed houses beneath towering red cliffs, with the iconic red phone box.
history: A small fishing village on the Moray Firth coast, sheltered beneath dramatic red sandstone cliffs. Made internationally famous by Bill Forsyth's 1983 film 'Local Hero' starring Burt Lancaster (though the phone box scenes were filmed at nearby Pennan rather than the main location). The village is reached by a steep single-track road. Beautiful and photogenic.

name: Castle Grant
description: The ancestral seat of Clan Grant near Grantown-on-Spey. A massive Z-plan castle with 15th-century origins, one of the largest in the Highlands.
history: Originally a Comyn stronghold known as Freuchie Castle, passing to the Grants in the 14th century. The present castle incorporates a 15th-century tower within extensive later additions. The Grant chiefs held the castle until the 20th century. James Boswell and Samuel Johnson stayed here in 1773. The castle is one of the largest in the Highlands. Private but visible from the road near Grantown-on-Spey.

name: Dalcross Castle
description: A restored 17th-century tower house near Inverness, built by the Fraser Lord Lovat. Compact Highland baronial architecture near the Moray Firth.
history: Built around 1620 by the Fraser Lord Lovat. A well-proportioned tower house with turrets and corbelling. Restored in the 20th century and now a private residence. Located near Dalcross, close to Inverness Airport and the Moray Firth. Part of the network of tower houses that dot the Black Isle and eastern Highlands.

name: Boath Doocot
description: A remarkably fine 17th-century doocot (dovecote) near Auldearn — one of Scotland's best-preserved, with nesting boxes for over 500 pigeons.
history: Built in the 17th century, this beehive-shaped doocot could house over 500 pigeons. Doocots were important sources of fresh meat and eggs in winter, and a mark of lairdly status. This example, near the site of the 1645 Battle of Auldearn, is one of the best-preserved in Scotland. Managed by Historic Environment Scotland.

name: Cromarty Courthouse
description: A beautifully restored 18th-century courthouse in the picturesque town of Cromarty, now a museum telling the story of this remarkable Black Isle town.
history: Built in 1773, the courthouse has been restored as a museum with interactive displays about Cromarty's history. Cromarty is one of Scotland's best-preserved 18th-century towns, birthplace of geologist Hugh Miller. The town features attractive Georgian streets, a harbour, and the Cromarty Firth. The courthouse museum provides an excellent introduction to the area's heritage.

name: Tarbat Discovery Centre
description: A museum in the restored Tarbat Old Church on the Easter Ross peninsula, displaying remarkable Pictish carved stones found during excavations.
history: The former church houses finds from archaeological excavations at the Tarbat peninsula, including spectacular Pictish carved stones and evidence of an important early medieval monastery. The site was a major Pictish ecclesiastical centre. The museum displays include intricately carved cross-slabs and fragments. Located in the village of Portmahomack.

name: Rosemarkie Pictish Stone
description: A remarkable Pictish cross-slab in Groam House Museum, Rosemarkie. Intricate 8th-century carving that reveals the artistic sophistication of the mysterious Picts.
history: The Rosemarkie cross-slab is an 8th-century Pictish carved stone, one of the finest examples of Pictish art in Scotland. Housed in Groam House Museum along with other Pictish fragments found locally. The carvings include elaborate interlace patterns, Pictish symbols, and Christian imagery, illustrating the cultural fusion of the period. Rosemarkie is a charming Black Isle village.

name: Dingwall Castle
description: Minimal remains of a royal castle in the Easter Ross town of Dingwall. Once an important seat of power, now reduced to a tower stump in the town centre.
history: A medieval royal castle, probably dating from the 12th century when Dingwall was an important centre of Norse-Scottish power in Easter Ross. Associated with Macbeth, who may have been born here. The castle was largely demolished, with only a tower stump surviving near the town centre. Dingwall itself was the birthplace of the real Macbeth and retains some historic character.

name: Fairburn Tower
description: A striking ruined tower house in Easter Ross, visible from the A835. The Mackenzie stronghold was the subject of a famous Brahan Seer prophecy.
history: Built in the 16th century by the Mackenzie family. A substantial tower house that dominates the landscape near Muir of Ord. The Brahan Seer prophesied that a cow would calve in the uppermost room — which reportedly happened when the ruin was used as a cattle shelter. Recently stabilised by Historic Environment Scotland. Visible and accessible from the road.

name: Redcastle
description: Ruins of a medieval castle on the Black Isle shore, overlooking the Beauly Firth. Ancient origins in a peaceful coastal setting.
history: One of several 'Red Castles' in Scotland, dating from the 12th or 13th century. Strategically positioned on the Black Isle shore overlooking the Beauly Firth. Associated with various Highland families including the Mackenzies. The ruins are modest but the coastal setting is attractive. Located near the village of Killearnan.

name: Dun Telve Broch
description: One of the best-preserved brochs on the Scottish mainland, standing over 10 metres high in Glen Beag near Glenelg. An awe-inspiring Iron Age tower in a remote Highland glen.
history: Built around 100 BC-100 AD, Dun Telve stands over 10 metres high — one of the tallest surviving brochs on the mainland. The double-walled construction is clearly visible. Located in remote Glen Beag near Glenelg, reached by a single-track road over the Mam Ratagan pass. Together with neighbouring Dun Troddan, these are among the finest broch remains in the Highlands.

name: Dun Troddan Broch
description: The twin of Dun Telve, standing nearby in Glen Beag — together they form the finest pair of mainland brochs in Scotland. Iron Age engineering at its most impressive.
history: Built around 100 BC-100 AD, Dun Troddan stands about 7.6 metres high, slightly shorter than its neighbour Dun Telve. The internal gallery structure is particularly well-preserved. Together, these two brochs form the most impressive group on the Scottish mainland. Located in Glen Beag near Glenelg, a remote corner of the western Highlands. Freely accessible.

name: Bernera Barracks
description: A ruined Hanoverian barracks near Glenelg, built to control the Highlands after the Jacobite risings. Where redcoats watched for rebels amid some of Scotland's finest scenery.
history: Built in 1723 as one of four barracks (with Fort George, Ruthven, and Inversnaid) to control the Highlands after the 1715 Jacobite rising. The barracks housed up to 200 soldiers. Garrisoned until after the 1745 rising. The ruins are substantial, standing in a magnificent setting near Glenelg with views across the Sound of Sleat to Skye. One of the best-preserved Hanoverian military buildings.

name: Creich Castle
description: A ruined tower house near Brunton in Fife, overlooking the Firth of Tay. Modest remains with fine estuary views.
history: A 15th or 16th-century tower house on the north shore of the Firth of Tay in Fife. The ruins are modest — mainly wall fragments — but the location overlooking the wide Tay estuary is attractive. Associated with various local families. One of many minor tower houses in the Fife countryside.

name: Rosyth Castle
description: A 15th-century tower house on a rocky outcrop now stranded within the Rosyth naval dockyard. A medieval castle marooned in an industrial landscape.
history: Built in the 15th century, probably by the Stewart family. Mary Queen of Scots is said to have stayed here as a child before sailing to France. The castle originally stood on a tidal island in the Firth of Forth but land reclamation for the Rosyth naval dockyard in 1909 left it landlocked. Now surrounded by industrial buildings, creating an incongruous setting.

name: Mauchline Castle
description: Remains of a medieval tower in the Ayrshire town where Robert Burns lived and married Jean Armour. Literary pilgrimage in Burns Country.
history: A medieval tower, now partly incorporated into later buildings in the town of Mauchline. The town is famous for its associations with Robert Burns, who lived here and married Jean Armour in 1788. Nearby attractions include Burns House Museum, Poosie Nansie's tavern (mentioned in Burns' works), and the churchyard where many of Burns' characters are buried.

name: Ayr Castle
description: Fragments of a once-important royal castle in the centre of Ayr. A major medieval fortress reduced to minimal traces in a busy town.
history: A significant royal castle from the 12th century, used by Scottish kings and captured by the English during the Wars of Independence. William Wallace burned the English garrison here in 1297. The castle was largely demolished over the centuries and very little remains visible in modern Ayr. The town itself retains historic character with the Auld Brig and connections to Robert Burns.

name: Sundrum Castle
description: A castle estate near Ayr with medieval origins, now a holiday park. Ancient walls wrapped in modern hospitality.
history: The estate dates from the 14th century, with the oldest part of the castle being a medieval tower house. Extended and remodelled over the centuries. Now operates as a holiday park with self-catering accommodation in the castle grounds. Set in attractive Ayrshire countryside near the River Doon.

name: Peel Ring of Lumphanan
description: A massive medieval earthwork in Aberdeenshire — one of the finest motte-and-bailey sites in Scotland. Where Macbeth met his end, according to tradition.
history: A 13th-century motte-and-bailey with impressive earthworks. Lumphanan is traditionally the site where Macbeth was killed in 1057, though the historical evidence is uncertain. The Peel Ring is an enormous circular earthwork, one of the largest and best-preserved in Scotland. Nearby is Macbeth's Cairn, marking the traditional site of his death. Managed by Historic Scotland.

name: Migvie Church
description: A ruined medieval church in Aberdeenshire with a notable Pictish carved stone. Simple rural ruin with an important sculptured monument.
history: A ruined medieval church in the parish of Tarland, notable for a Pictish symbol stone built into the wall — featuring a cross and Pictish symbols. The church dates from the medieval period but the carved stone is much older. Set in the rural landscape of upper Donside. Of interest primarily for the Pictish stone.

name: Sueno's Stone
description: The largest and most elaborate Pictish carved stone in Scotland — a towering 6.5-metre monolith depicting a great battle. An enigmatic masterpiece of Dark Age art, enclosed in glass.
history: A massive 9th or 10th-century carved cross-slab, standing 6.5 metres tall. One side shows an elaborate cross with interlace; the other depicts a detailed battle scene with ranks of warriors, cavalry, and decapitated enemies. The stone's purpose and the battle it commemorates remain debated. Protected within a glass enclosure beside the B9011 near Forres. A scheduled ancient monument of international importance.

name: Burghead Well
description: A mysterious early Christian or Pictish well chamber cut deep into rock at Burghead — a unique subterranean structure whose purpose continues to puzzle archaeologists.
history: A rock-cut chamber reached by a flight of steps, containing a basin filled by a natural spring. Located within the site of a massive Pictish fort at Burghead on the Moray coast. The well's purpose is debated — baptismal pool, ritual well, or water supply. Burghead was one of the most important Pictish fortresses in Scotland. The annual 'Burning of the Clavie' fire festival continues here.

name: Dallas Dhu Distillery
description: A perfectly preserved Victorian malt whisky distillery near Forres, frozen in time as a museum. Smell the malt, see the stills, and understand how Scotland's national drink is made.
history: Built in 1898 and operational until 1983, Dallas Dhu is now preserved as a museum of whisky-making by Historic Environment Scotland. The distillery retains all its original equipment — kiln, mash tun, washbacks, and copper stills — giving visitors a complete picture of traditional malt whisky production. One of Scotland's most authentic whisky heritage experiences.

name: Rothiemay Castle
description: A private castle in the Deveron valley, Banffshire. Medieval origins with later baronial additions, set in the rolling Moray countryside.
history: Dating from the 15th century, held by the Forbes and later Gordon families. The castle incorporates medieval elements with later extensions. Set in the scenic Deveron valley near the village of Rothiemay. Private residence. The area is known for its productive farmland and fishing rivers.

name: Blairfindy Castle
description: A ruined L-plan tower house in the heart of Speyside whisky country, near Glenlivet. Medieval ruins amid Scotland's most famous distilling landscape.
history: Built around 1586 by the Gordon family. An L-plan tower house that was burned by Jacobite forces in 1689. The ruins stand in a field near the Glenlivet Distillery in the heart of Speyside. The castle offers atmospheric ruins in a beautiful Highland setting, easily combined with a whisky distillery visit.

name: Inchdrewer Castle
description: A ruined 16th-century tower house near Banff on the Moray Firth coast. Atmospheric ruin with a chequered modern history of failed restoration attempts.
history: Built in the 16th century by the Ogilvie family. A substantial L-plan tower house that has been roofless since the 18th century. In recent decades, various owners have attempted restoration without success, making it one of Scotland's most contentious heritage sites. The ruins are impressive, standing near the coast at Banff.

name: Boyne Castle
description: Impressive ruins of a large courtyard castle near Portsoy in Banffshire. One of the largest medieval castles in northeast Scotland, surprisingly little-known.
history: Built in the 16th century by the Ogilvie family, Boyne Castle is one of the largest castles in northeast Scotland. A substantial courtyard castle with towers and ranges of buildings. The ruins are extensive but overgrown, hidden in woodland near the coast. Portsoy itself is a charming harbour town famous for its marble. The castle is freely accessible but requires some path-finding.

name: Fordyce Castle
description: A charming 16th-century tower house in the perfectly preserved village of Fordyce in Banffshire. A fairy-tale tower in a fairy-tale village.
history: Built in 1592, the castle stands in the remarkably well-preserved village of Fordyce. The village itself is a conservation gem with 16th-19th century buildings grouped around a medieval church. The castle is a compact L-plan tower house. Fordyce was designated an Outstanding Conservation Area. The village and castle together form one of northeast Scotland's most charming heritage sites.

name: Deskford Church
description: A ruined medieval church in Banffshire, notable for a fine sacrament house — one of the best examples of pre-Reformation church furnishing in northeast Scotland.
history: A medieval parish church with a carved sacrament house dated to the early 16th century. The sacrament house features delicate Gothic tracery and an inscription. The original is now in the National Museum of Scotland, with a cast in situ. The church ruins are atmospheric, set in a quiet Banffshire churchyard. The famous Deskford Carnyx (a Celtic war trumpet) was found nearby.

name: Edinkillie Church
description: A ruined medieval church near Dunphail in Moray, with fine carved stones in a peaceful woodland setting.
history: A medieval parish church in a remote Moray setting, with carved grave slabs and fragments. The church has been ruined since at least the 18th century. Set in woodland near the River Divie, in a landscape of great natural beauty. Of interest primarily for its medieval carved stones and atmospheric setting.

name: Tower of Hallbar
description: A restored 16th-century tower house near Crossford in Lanarkshire, now a Landmark Trust holiday let. A compact fortress brought beautifully back to life.
history: Built in the late 16th century, a simple rectangular tower house. Fell into disrepair but was restored by the Landmark Trust as holiday accommodation. Features a barrel-vaulted ground floor, turnpike stair, and rooftop parapet walk. Set in the Clyde valley near Crossford. One of the Landmark Trust's most popular Scottish properties.

name: Lincluden Collegiate Church
description: Exquisite late Gothic ruins near Dumfries — the carved stonework of the Douglas princess's tomb is among the finest medieval sculpture in Scotland.
history: Founded as a Benedictine nunnery in the 12th century, refounded as a collegiate church by Archibald 'the Grim', 3rd Earl of Douglas, around 1389. The tomb of Princess Margaret Douglas features extraordinary carved tracery — considered the finest surviving medieval tomb sculpture in Scotland. Robert Burns admired the ruins and wrote about them. Managed by Historic Environment Scotland.

name: Capenoch Tower
description: A small tower house ruin in rural Dumfriesshire, part of the network of minor fortified houses that characterised the lawless Borders region.
history: A small medieval tower house in the Dumfriesshire countryside. Part of the dense network of towers and peel houses that defended against raiding in the Borders. Limited remains survive. The surrounding landscape is peaceful agricultural country in upper Nithsdale.

name: Hoddom Castle
description: A substantial 16th-century tower house near Ecclefechan in Dumfriesshire. A Maxwell stronghold in the debatable lands between Scotland and England.
history: Built around 1565 by John Maxwell, 4th Lord Herries, a supporter of Mary Queen of Scots. A large and impressive tower house with later additions. Set in the Annandale countryside near the childhood home of Thomas Carlyle at Ecclefechan. The castle was once surrounded by extensive policies. Now privately owned and used for events.

name: Repentance Tower
description: A mysterious hilltop tower near Hoddom in Dumfriesshire, built as an act of penitence by Lord Herries. The word 'REPENTANCE' is carved above the door.
history: Built around 1565 by John Maxwell, 4th Lord Herries — the same builder as Hoddom Castle — reportedly as an act of penance (for what sin is debated). The word 'REPENTANCE' is inscribed above the entrance. Standing on a hilltop, it also served as a watchtower and beacon. A unique and enigmatic monument in the Dumfriesshire landscape.

name: Lockerbie Tower
description: A small tower ruin in the Dumfriesshire town of Lockerbie. Medieval remnant in a town known for tragedy.
history: A medieval tower in the town of Lockerbie, Dumfriesshire. Little survives. The town is best known for the 1988 Pan Am Flight 103 bombing — a memorial garden commemorates the 270 victims. The tower is of mainly local historical interest.

name: Peebles Cross Kirk
description: Atmospheric ruins of a medieval friary in the Borders town of Peebles, with a remarkable decorated west tower. Founded on the site of a miraculous discovery of a holy cross.
history: Founded in 1261 after the reported discovery of a magnificent cross and the relics of St Nicholas. A Trinitarian friary, one of the rarest monastic orders in Scotland. The west tower has fine decorated Gothic stonework. The friary was an important pilgrimage site. The ruins stand peacefully beside the River Tweed in Peebles. Managed by Historic Environment Scotland.

name: Coldingham Priory
description: A medieval priory church in the Berwickshire coastal village of Coldingham, partly restored and still in use. Ancient monastic foundation with beautiful stonework.
history: Founded in 1098 by Edgar, King of Scotland, on the site of a 7th-century Anglo-Saxon monastery. The priory was a dependency of Durham Cathedral and frequently fought over during Anglo-Scottish wars. Partly demolished at the Reformation but the choir was restored and remains in use as the parish church. Features fine Gothic arcading. Near the coastal village of Coldingham with its sandy bay.

name: Lennoxlove House
description: Seat of the Duke of Hamilton in East Lothian, housing an important art collection including a death mask of Mary Queen of Scots. Baronial grandeur near Haddington.
history: A tower house dating from the 14th century, extensively extended. Named 'Lennoxlove' by Frances Stewart, Duchess of Richmond — La Belle Stuart, model for Britannia on British coins. Houses the Hamilton Collection including Mary Queen of Scots' death mask and a sapphire ring. The grounds include parkland and gardens. Open for guided tours.

name: Preston Tower
description: A restored 15th-century tower house near Prestonpans in East Lothian. A Hamilton stronghold with views to the Firth of Forth and the site of the 1745 Battle of Prestonpans.
history: Built around 1454 by the Hamilton family. Burned by the English in 1544 and 1650. Restored in the 20th century. The tower stands near the site of the 1745 Battle of Prestonpans, where Bonnie Prince Charlie won his first significant victory. Features a barrel-vaulted ground floor, turnpike stair, and rooftop views. Open to visitors.

name: Castle Levan
description: A ruined tower house near Gourock, overlooking the Firth of Clyde. Once a Morton stronghold, now a crumbling relic in the Inverclyde landscape.
history: A 15th-century tower house built by the Morton family, overlooking the Clyde near Gourock. The tower survives to a reasonable height but is roofless and deteriorating. Views across the Firth of Clyde to the Argyll hills.

name: Lennox Castle
description: A vast ruined Victorian Gothic castle near Lennoxtown, once a psychiatric hospital. An enormous and eerie shell in the Campsie Fells.
history: Built 1837-41 for John Lennox Kincaid in elaborate Tudor Gothic style. From 1927 to 2002 it served as Lennox Castle Hospital, a psychiatric facility. Severely damaged by fire in 2008. The enormous shell is among the largest castle ruins in Scotland.

name: Kilmacolm Castle
description: Minimal remains of a medieval castle in the village of Kilmacolm, Renfrewshire.
history: A medieval castle site in the village of Kilmacolm. Very little survives above ground. Of mainly local archaeological interest.

name: Cathcart Castle
description: Fragmentary ruins of a medieval castle in Linn Park, Glasgow. A rare medieval survivor within the city boundaries.
history: A 15th-century castle of the Cathcart family above the White Cart Water. Mary Queen of Scots may have watched the Battle of Langside from this area in 1568. Fragments within Linn Park, one of Glasgow's largest parks.

name: Mearns Castle
description: A restored 15th-century tower house in Newton Mearns, incorporated into a church. Medieval fortress meets modern worship in suburban Glasgow.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Maxwell family. Incorporated into Mearns Kirk in 1813, creating an unusual combination of medieval fortress and church.

name: Cardross Manor
description: Site where Robert the Bruce spent his final years and died in 1329. Almost nothing survives, but the historical significance is immense.
history: Robert the Bruce died here on 7 June 1329. Almost nothing survives above ground. A monument marks the approximate location near the village of Cardross.

name: Arnprior Tower
description: Ruins of a small tower house near Arnprior in Stirlingshire. A Buchanan stronghold whose laird was known as the 'King of Kippen'.
history: A 16th-century tower house built by the Buchanan family. The laird boldly stopped a supply train intended for James V. Only fragments survive in farmland.

name: Sauchie Tower
description: A well-preserved 15th-century tower house near Alloa, standing amid modern housing. A medieval survivor in an unlikely setting.
history: Built in the 15th century by the Schaw family. James IV visited in 1504. Well-preserved with a vaulted ground floor. Surrounded by modern housing in Sauchie.

name: Dollar Academy Clock Tower
description: The landmark clock tower of Dollar Academy, designed by William Henry Playfair. One of Scotland's finest Greek Revival buildings.
history: Dollar Academy was founded in 1818 by John McNabb. The main building with clock tower was designed by Playfair. One of the finest examples of Greek Revival architecture in Scotland.

name: Kilberry Sculptured Stones
description: A collection of medieval West Highland carved stones near Kilberry in Argyll. Masterpieces of Gaelic memorial art.
history: Medieval carved stones from the 14th-16th centuries, featuring warriors, galleys, crosses, and foliage in the West Highland tradition. Displayed in a roadside shelter on the remote Knapdale coast.

name: Ballinaby Standing Stones
description: Bronze Age standing stones on the west coast of Islay, part of the island's extraordinary prehistoric landscape.
history: A group of Bronze Age standing stones on the Rhinns of Islay. Part of an extraordinary concentration of prehistoric monuments on Scotland's whisky island.

name: Keills Chapel
description: A tiny medieval chapel on the remote Knapdale coast housing a superb 8th-century cross. An atmospheric gem at the end of a single-track road.
history: Contains Keills Cross, an outstanding 8th-century free-standing cross, and medieval West Highland carved stones. Overlooking the Sound of Jura at the end of a single-track road.

name: Kildalton Cross
description: The finest intact high cross in Scotland â€” an 8th-century masterpiece of Celtic art on Islay. Breathtaking craftsmanship.
history: Dating from around 800 AD, featuring biblical scenes and Celtic interlace. Made from local epidiorite stone, possibly by an Iona-trained sculptor. Standing 2.65 metres tall in Kildalton Old Parish Church churchyard.

name: Campbeltown Cross
description: A fine 15th-century medieval market cross in Kintyre, carved with Celtic interlace and a crucifixion scene.
history: A 15th-century carved market cross in the West Highland tradition. Campbeltown was the main town of Kintyre and centre of the Lordship of the Isles.

name: St Blane's Church
description: Beautifully sited early medieval monastery ruin on the Isle of Bute. Celtic Christian ruins in an idyllic island setting.
history: Founded in the 6th century by St Blane. Ruins include a 12th-century Romanesque chapel and earlier enclosures with divided burial grounds. Reached by a walk on Bute's southern tip.

name: Innischonain Priory
description: Ruins of an Augustinian priory on an island in Loch Awe. Monastic solitude on one of Argyll's most beautiful lochs.
history: A small 13th-century Augustinian priory on the island of Inishail in Loch Awe. Also an ancient burial ground for Campbells and MacArthurs. Accessible only by boat.

name: Temple Church
description: Ruins of a medieval church in Midlothian, originally built by the Knights Templar. Scotland's principal Templar site.
history: The village of Temple was the principal Scottish preceptory of the Knights Templar from the 12th century. The ruined church dates from the 14th century, built by the Hospitallers.

name: Arniston House
description: A magnificent William Adam mansion in Midlothian, still owned by the Dundas family. One of Scotland's finest Georgian houses.
history: Designed by William Adam around 1726 for Robert Dundas, Lord Arniston. Outstanding Palladian interiors. Open for guided tours on selected days.

name: Lochnagar Bothie
description: A mountain bothy beneath the cliffs of Lochnagar in the Cairngorms. Shelter for hillwalkers in dramatic mountain scenery.
history: A stone shelter beneath Lochnagar (1,155m). The mountain inspired Byron and Prince Charles. Within the Balmoral estate and Cairngorms National Park.

name: Durris Castle
description: A private castle in the Dee valley near Banchory, with medieval origins. Hidden in woodlands above Royal Deeside.
history: A castle with medieval origins, extensively remodelled. Set in woodland above the Dee valley. Private residence.

name: Raemoir House
description: A country house near Banchory on Royal Deeside. Georgian elegance in the beautiful Dee valley.
history: A Georgian country house with earlier origins near Banchory. Has been used as a hotel. In the heart of Royal Deeside.

name: Druminnor Castle
description: A restored medieval castle near Rhynie in Aberdeenshire, seat of the Forbes family for over 500 years.
history: Oldest part dates from the 15th century with a rare surviving medieval great hall. Forbes family has held it for over 500 years. Private, occasionally opens for garden visits.

name: Torphichen Preceptory
description: The only surviving medieval headquarters of the Knights Hospitaller in Scotland. Unique vaulted interiors in West Lothian.
history: Founded in the 12th century as the Scottish HQ of the Knights of St John. Remarkable stone vaulting in surviving transepts. The 'refuge stone' in the churchyard marked sanctuary boundaries.

name: Cairnpapple Hill
description: A major prehistoric ceremonial site in West Lothian spanning 3,000 years. Scotland's most important neolithic-bronze age ritual landscape.
history: Used from around 3000 BC to 1400 BC. Features Neolithic henge, Bronze Age burial cairns, and panoramic hilltop views from the Highlands to the Borders.

name: Kinneil House
description: A historic mansion in Bo'ness with rare Renaissance painted ceilings. Where James Watt tested his steam engine.
history: 15th-century Hamilton residence with remarkable Renaissance painted ceilings (c.1553) and 17th-century murals. James Watt tested his steam engine in an outbuilding here. Set in Kinneil Estate public park.

name: Kinfauns Castle
description: A Gothic Revival mansion above the Tay near Perth, designed by Robert Smirke. Now luxury apartments.
history: Built 1822-26 by Robert Smirke for Lord Gray. Converted to luxury residential apartments. Magnificent views across the Tay valley.

name: Fowlis Wester Sculptured Stone
description: A magnificent 8th-century Pictish cross-slab in a tiny Perthshire village church, featuring a procession of figures.
history: An 8th-century Pictish cross-slab standing over 3 metres tall, housed inside Fowlis Wester church. Features elaborate carvings including a robed procession, animals, and Pictish symbols.

name: Aberlemno Sculptured Stones
description: A remarkable group of Pictish carved stones in Angus â€” including a famous battle scene believed to depict the Battle of Nechtansmere.
history: Four Pictish carved stones (7th-9th century) in Aberlemno village. The churchyard stone depicts what scholars believe is the Battle of Nechtansmere (685 AD). Among Scotland's most important Pictish monuments.

name: Tealing Doocot
description: A fine 16th-century doocot near Dundee with over 500 nesting boxes. A well-preserved example of these Scottish agricultural buildings.
history: 16th-century beehive-shaped doocot with over 500 nesting boxes. Adjacent to ruins of a medieval chapel and nearby souterrain.

name: Ethie Castle
description: A 14th-century castle near Arbroath associated with Cardinal Beaton and rumoured to be haunted. Private residence.
history: 14th-century castle with connections to Cardinal David Beaton. Haunted reputation â€” a child's skeleton was reportedly found walled up. Overlooks the Angus coast near Arbroath.

name: Cortachy Castle
description: Seat of the Earls of Airlie in Glen Clova, Angus. Haunted by a ghostly drummer who heralds death.
history: Home of the Ogilvy Earls of Airlie since the 15th century. Famous for the Airlie Drummer legend. Set at the entrance to the Angus Glens.

name: Airlie Castle
description: The original Ogilvy stronghold, burned by Argyll in 1640. The destruction inspired the ballad 'The Bonnie House of Airlie'.
history: 15th-century castle burned during the Covenanting wars in 1640 while Lady Airlie was in residence. The ballad immortalised the event. The family seat moved to nearby Cortachy.

name: Kinnaird Castle
description: A grand baronial castle near Brechin, seat of the Carnegie Earls of Southesk. Victorian grandeur in rolling Angus countryside.
history: Carnegie family seat since the 15th century. Current castle rebuilt 1854 by David Bryce in Scottish baronial style. Extensive parkland. Private.

name: House of Dun
description: An elegant Georgian house near Montrose designed by William Adam, with superb rococo plasterwork. National Trust for Scotland.
history: Built 1730 by William Adam for David Erskine. Features extraordinary rococo plasterwork by Joseph Enzer. Gardens, woodland walks, and Victorian playground. NTS property.

name: St Vigeans Museum
description: A small museum near Arbroath housing outstanding Pictish carved stones, including the Drosten Stone.
history: Over 30 carved stones from the 7th-10th centuries. The Drosten Stone features what may be the earliest known Scots language inscription. Managed by Historic Environment Scotland.

name: Balintore Castle
description: A fairy-tale Scottish baronial castle in Angus, designed by William Burn. Being rescued from ruin by passionate conservationists.
history: Built 1859 for David Lyon MP. Fell into severe ruin. Being painstakingly restored since 2007. A remarkable conservation rescue story.

name: Pitcur Souterrain
description: An Iron Age underground passage near Dundee â€” one of the finest souterrains in Scotland. A mysterious 2,000-year-old tunnel.
history: Well-preserved Iron Age souterrain (c.100 BC - 200 AD) curving for about 55 metres underground. Purpose (storage, ritual, refuge) remains debated.

name: Ardestie Souterrain
description: Another excellent Iron Age underground passage near Dundee. Twin tunnels from Scotland's mysterious prehistoric past.
history: Iron Age souterrain near Monifieth (c.100 BC - 200 AD). Together with Pitcur, among the finest souterrains in Scotland.

name: Caterthun Hill Forts
description: Spectacular twin Iron Age hillforts on adjacent Angus hills â€” the White and Brown Caterthuns. Among Scotland's finest prehistoric fortifications.
history: Two major Iron Age hillforts near Edzell. The White Caterthun has a massive stone rampart. The Brown has multiple concentric earthworks. Views from Cairngorms to North Sea.

name: Piperdam Resort
description: A modern leisure resort near Dundee set in woodland around a loch. Popular base for exploring Angus and Perthshire heritage.
history: A modern leisure resort complex near Fowlis, Angus. Features self-catering lodges, golf, spa, and outdoor activities.

name: Fowlis Easter Church
description: A medieval church near Dundee with rare pre-Reformation painted panels â€” Scotland's finest surviving medieval church paintings.
history: Built in 1453, retains rare pre-Reformation painted panels depicting the Crucifixion. Also features a carved sacrament house and medieval font. Still a parish church.

name: Mains Castle Dundee
description: A restored 16th-century tower house in suburban Dundee, converted into residential flats. Medieval living in a modern city.
history: Built around 1562 by David Graham. Restored in the 1980s and converted into apartments. Located in Caird Park area of Dundee.

name: Powrie Castle
description: A ruined tower house on the outskirts of Dundee.
history: A 16th-century tower house near the village of Powrie. Held by various local families. Fragmentary ruins in farmland.
